llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces a personalized federated learning framework with user-level joint differential privacy (JDP) and shows that tuning the level of personalization improves the privacyвҖ“accuracy tradeoff. Proposes Personalized-Private-SGD (PPSGD) with a personalization parameter Оұ controlling the relative weight of global vs. local updates. Derives finite-sample generalization bounds decomposing excess risk into a statistical term that decreases with per-user samples n and a privacy term independent of n, demonstrating that personalization can attenuate the privacy cost. Extends analysis to client sampling and heterogeneous mini-batches, providing privacy (via subsampling amplification) and generalization guarantees, plus a criterion (threshold on n) for when personalization is beneficial. Validates theory with experiments on synthetic data, StackOverflow, and EMNIST (linear and CNN).
llm_extracted_info.methodology: Formulates a stochastic optimization objective over a global parameter w and per-user local parameters Оё_i; key additive model: в„“(y, (w+Оё_i)^T x). Proposes PPSGD (Algorithm 1): each round, every user updates Оё_i locally with step О·/N, sends a clipped gradient wrt w; server aggregates, adds Gaussian noise, and updates w with step ОұО·. Ensures (Оө,Оҙ)-JDP in the billboard model by clipping and Gaussian mechanism; noise scale Пғ_О¶ вүҘ c C sqrt(n log(1/Оҙ)) /(N Оө). Provides convex, L-smooth, bounded-gradient analysis using a preconditioned SGD view with Оұ-dependent geometry; establishes excess risk bounds: bias O(L_ОұвҲҘz*вҲҘ_Оұ^2/n), variance O(вҲҘz*вҲҘ_Оұ Пғ_tot,Оұ/вҲҡn), with privacy term Оұ d_w Пғ_О¶^2 independent of n. Extends to PPSGD with client sampling (Algorithm 2): users sampled with prob q and minibatch sizes m_i; updates scaled by 1/(qM); privacy amplified with Пғ_О¶ вүҘ c C sqrt(T log(1/Оҙ)) /(M Оө); generalization bound includes extra variance from client sampling and privacy. Provides Algorithm 3 to optimize unweighted average user risk by averaging per-user gradients, yielding privacy cost similar to homogeneous case. Derives a closed-form Оұ-norm minimizer and a threshold on n for when increasing personalization reduces variance.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: Code released (URL in paper). Privacy accounting via moments accountant with Оҙ=1e-4; Gaussian noise added after per-user gradient clipping; step-size О· optimized per iteration over a grid; Оұ varied over a grid; noise multiplier Пғ varied over a grid. Synthetic regression: N=1000, d=100; partially shared user parameters; x~N(0, diag(1/k)); label noise П„^2; one pass, Q=N users per round, m=10 per user, C=10; evaluate closed-form excess risk. StackOverflow tag prediction: subset with N=500 users, 300вҖ“500 train and 10вҖ“100 test docs/user; 5000-dim bag-of-words; 80 most frequent tags (one-vs-all); one pass, Q=10, m=10, C=0.01; report top-1 test accuracy and Оө over iterations. EMNIST: N=1000 users, ~100 train, ~10 test/user. Linear model: 20 passes, Q=10, m=10, C=10. CNN: 4-layer CNN with global representation ОҰ_W and additive output layer; 20 passes, Q=10, m=10, C=0.1. Across datasets, compare local-only (Оұ=0), global-only (ОұвҶ’вҲһ), and intermediate Оұ; plot accuracy/excess risk vs Оө; show best personalized curve by optimizing Оұ and О·.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: Theory assumes convex, L-smooth losses and bounded gradients (or clipping with CвүҘG), which may not hold in deep non-convex models; non-convex results are empirical only. Privacy analysis relies on joint DP in the billboard model and assumes users do not reveal their local models; practical leakage via local outputs is not addressed. Bounds depend on dimension via Оұ d_w and on clipping/gradient norm G, which can be large; clipping effects on optimization are not analyzed tightly. Algorithms are synchronous and assume access to fresh samples; system heterogeneity, stragglers, and communication constraints are not modeled. Heterogeneous data sizes increase privacy cost (scales with m_max/M); tradeoffs between optimizing weighted vs unweighted risks can hurt statistical efficiency. No comparison with stronger baselines (e.g., DP-FedAvg, advanced personalization architectures) in depth; hyperparameter selection (Оұ, C, Пғ) requires tuning. Experiments are limited to linear models and one CNN, a few datasets, and do not explore robustness or fairness.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: Extend theoretical guarantees to non-convex settings (deep models, learned representations) and analyze clipping impacts. Develop adaptive/online schemes to tune Оұ based on data size, heterogeneity, and target Оө. Integrate and analyze local SGD/FedAvg-style personalization under JDP with tighter bounds. Explore richer personalization structures (e.g., shared representations, factorized models, per-feature global/local gating) with privacy. Optimize client sampling and weighting to jointly minimize privacy cost and variance under heterogeneity. Improve privacy accounting (e.g., RDP/PRV accountants) and noise calibration; consider alternative DP notions (subsampled RDP, concentrated DP). Study fairness and per-user performance guarantees under personalization with privacy. Evaluate on broader benchmarks and real deployments with communication/compute constraints and partial participation. Investigate per-coordinate clipping/noise and structured noise to reduce dimension dependence. Design mechanisms preventing leakage from local models while retaining personalization benefits.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_code:
# ===== Core PPSGD for linear models on real datasets (from main_real.py) =====
from opacus.accountants.rdp import RDPAccountant
import numpy as np
import time

def training_priv(ds, step=0.5, stepw=None, steptheta=None,
                  C=10., noise_mult=0.05, delta=1e-4, eval_delta=500):
    # Global and local parameters
    w = np.zeros((ds.d, ds.n_classes))                  # global parameter w
    thetas = np.zeros((ds.N, ds.d, ds.n_classes))       # local parameters theta_i

    # Privacy accountant (RDP composition + conversion to (eps, delta))
    priv = RDPAccountant()

    if stepw is None:
        stepw = step
    if steptheta is None:
        steptheta = step

    ls = []
    accs = []
    accs_test = []
    test_iters = []
    epss = []

    for i, (ids, X, y) in enumerate(ds.batches()):
        L = len(ids)                 # number of users this round
        X = X[:,:L,:]                # [B, N, d]
        y = y[:,:L,:]                # [B, N, K]
        y -= 0.1                     # label centering (linearized squared loss)

        # Predictions under additive model: l(y, (w + theta_i)^T x)
        pred = np.sum(X[:,:,:,None] * (w[None,None,:,:] + thetas[None,ids,:,:]), axis=2)

        # Periodic evaluation (optional)
        if i % eval_delta == 0:
            print(i, 'eval test acc...', end='', flush=True)
            t = time.time()
            acc_test = ds.eval_test(w, thetas)
            print(acc_test, '({:.2f})'.format(time.time() - t))
            test_iters.append(i)
            accs_test.append(acc_test)

        # Per-user gradients of local parameters (unclipped)
        thetas_grad = np.sum(X[:,:,:,None] * (pred - y)[:,:,None,:], axis=0) / ds.batch_size  # [N, d, K]

        # Local updates (noisy-free, fully local)
        if steptheta > 0:
            thetas[ids] -= steptheta * thetas_grad

        # Per-user clipping for billboard-JDP: clip each user's contribution by L2 norm C
        norms = np.linalg.norm(thetas_grad, axis=(1,2))    # per-user
        scale = np.clip(norms / C, a_min=1., a_max=None)   # divisor >= 1
        thetas_grad /= scale[:,None,None]

        # Server-side aggregation of global gradient (average of clipped local grads)
        w_grad = np.mean(thetas_grad, axis=0)              # [d, K]

        # Gaussian mechanism for global update (noise independent across coords)
        if stepw > 0:
            noise = noise_mult * C * np.random.randn(ds.d, ds.n_classes) / L
            w -= stepw * (w_grad + noise)

            # Privacy accounting uses sampling rate q = L / N
            priv.step(noise_multiplier=noise_mult, sample_rate=L/ds.N)

        # Track privacy loss (epsilon at delta)
        eps, best_alpha = priv.get_privacy_spent(delta=delta)
        if i % eval_delta == 0:
            print('eps:', eps)
        epss.append(eps)

    return {'ls': ls, 'accs': accs, 'test_iters': test_iters, 'accs_test': accs_test, 'epss': epss}

# ===== Synthetic linear data + PPSGD (from main_synthetic.py) =====
from opacus.accountants.rdp import RDPAccountant
import numpy as np

class Dataset(object):
    def __init__(self, d=20, N=1000, user_feats=None, local_weight=0.01, seed=42):
        self.d = d
        self.N = N
        self.rd = np.random.RandomState(seed)
        mu0 = 10. * self.rd.randn(d)
        if user_feats is None:
            user_feats = (-5, None)
        mask = np.zeros(d)
        mask[user_feats[0]:user_feats[1]] = 1.
        self.theta0 = mu0[None,:]  + local_weight * self.rd.randn(N, d) * mask[None,:]
        self.var = np.array(1. / (1. + np.arange(d)))
        self.sigma = 0.01

    def get_batch(self, b=10):
        X = self.rd.randn(b, self.N, self.d) * np.sqrt(self.var)[None,None,:]
        y = np.sum(X * self.theta0[None,:,:], axis=2) + self.sigma * self.rd.randn(b, self.N)
        return X, y

    def excess_risk(self, w, thetas):
        return np.mean(self.var[None,:] * ((w[None,:] + thetas) - self.theta0) ** 2)


def training_priv(ds, batch_size=10,
                  step=0.5, stepw=None, steptheta=None,
                  niter=300, C=10., noise_mult=0.05, sample_rate=1., delta=1e-4):
    w = np.zeros(ds.d)
    thetas = np.zeros((ds.N, ds.d))

    priv = RDPAccountant()

    if stepw is None:
        stepw = step
    if steptheta is None:
        steptheta = step

    ls = []
    epss = []
    for i in range(niter):
        X, y = ds.get_batch(batch_size)                   # X:[B,N,d], y:[B,N]
        pred = np.sum(X * (w[None,:] + thetas), axis=2)   # additive model

        thetas_grad = np.sum(X * (pred - y)[:,:,None], axis=0) / batch_size  # [N,d]
        if steptheta > 0:
            thetas -= steptheta * thetas_grad             # local update

        # Clip per-user contributions by C
        norms = np.linalg.norm(thetas_grad, axis=1)
        thetas_grad /= np.clip(norms / C, a_min=1., a_max=None)[:,None]

        # Aggregate and privatize global gradient
        w_grad = np.mean(thetas_grad, axis=0)
        if stepw > 0:
            noise = noise_mult * C * np.random.randn(ds.d) / ds.N
            w -= stepw * (w_grad + noise)
            priv.step(noise_multiplier=noise_mult, sample_rate=1.)

        ls.append(ds.excess_risk(w, thetas))
        eps, best_alpha = priv.get_privacy_spent(delta=delta)
        epss.append(eps)
    return ls, epss

# ===== CNN variant in JAX (optional; same PPSGD principle, from main_nn.py) =====
from opacus.accountants.rdp import RDPAccountant
from jax import grad, jit, random, vmap
from jax.example_libraries import stax
from jax.tree_util import tree_flatten, tree_unflatten
import jax.numpy as jnp
import numpy as np

fdim = 32
init_random_params, predict_features = stax.serial(
    stax.Conv(16, (8, 8), padding='SAME', strides=(2, 2)),
    stax.Relu,
    stax.MaxPool((2, 2), (1, 1)),
    stax.Conv(32, (4, 4), padding='VALID', strides=(2, 2)),
    stax.Relu,
    stax.MaxPool((2, 2), (1, 1)),
    stax.Flatten,
    stax.Dense(fdim),
    stax.Relu,
)

def predict(params, w, theta, x):
    features = predict_features(params, x)
    return jnp.sum(features[:,:,None] * (w[None,:,:] + theta[None,:,:]), axis=1)

def loss(params, w, theta, x, y):
    return jnp.mean((predict(params, w, theta, x) - y) ** 2)

# Per-user gradient, per-user clipping on combined gradient (params + w), no clip on theta-grad

def clipped_grad(params, w, theta, l2_norm_clip, x, y):
    gp, gw, gth = grad(loss, (0, 1, 2))(params, w, theta, x, y)
    grads, tree_def = tree_flatten(gp)
    total_grad_norm = jnp.sqrt(jnp.sum(gw ** 2) + jnp.array([jnp.sum(g.ravel() ** 2) for g in grads]).sum())
    divisor = jnp.maximum(total_grad_norm / l2_norm_clip, 1.)
    normalized_grads = [g / divisor for g in grads]
    return tree_unflatten(tree_def, normalized_grads), gw / divisor, gth

@jit
def private_grad(params, w, thetas, xs, ys, rng, l2_norm_clip, noise_mult, num_users):
    cgp, cgw, gth = vmap(clipped_grad, (None, None, 0, None, 1, 1))(
        params, w, thetas, l2_norm_clip, xs, ys)
    # Aggregate across users
    cgp_flat, grads_treedef = tree_flatten(cgp)
    cgp_tot = [g.mean(0) for g in cgp_flat]
    rngp, rngw = random.split(rng)
    rngs = random.split(rngp, len(cgp_tot))
    # Add Gaussian noise to aggregated params and w gradients
    cgp_noised = [g + l2_norm_clip * noise_mult * random.normal(r, g.shape) / num_users
                  for r, g in zip(rngs, cgp_tot)]
    cgw_tot = cgw.mean(0)
    cgw_noised = cgw_tot + l2_norm_clip * noise_mult * random.normal(rngw, cgw_tot.shape) / num_users
    return tree_unflatten(grads_treedef, cgp_noised), cgw_noised, gth

@jit
def update_params(params, params_grad, stepw):
    ps, tree_def = tree_flatten(params)
    gs, _ = tree_flatten(params_grad)
    ps_new = [p - stepw * g for (p, g) in zip(ps, gs)]
    return tree_unflatten(tree_def, ps_new)

# Top-level PPSGD loop (CNN features + global linear head w + local theta)

def training_priv_cnn(ds, step=0.5, stepw=None, steptheta=None,
                      C=10., noise_mult=0.05, delta=1e-4, eval_delta=500):
    key = random.PRNGKey(42)
    _, params = init_random_params(key, (-1, 28, 28, 1))
    w = np.random.randn(fdim, ds.n_classes) / np.sqrt(fdim)
    thetas = np.zeros((ds.N, fdim, ds.n_classes))

    priv = RDPAccountant()

    if stepw is None:
        stepw = step
    if steptheta is None:
        steptheta = step

    accs_test, test_iters, epss = [], [], []

    def eval_test(params, w, thetas):
        acc = 0
        cnt = 0
        for i, (X, y) in enumerate(ds.test_datasets):
            preds = predict(params, w, thetas[i], X.reshape(-1, 28, 28, 1))
            acc += np.sum(preds.argmax(1) == y)
            cnt += len(y)
        return acc / cnt

    for i, (ids, X, y) in enumerate(ds.batches()):
        L = len(ids)
        X = X[:,:L,:].reshape(X.shape[0], L, 28, 28, 1)
        y = y[:,:L,:]
        y -= 0.1
        if i % eval_delta == 0:
            acc_test = eval_test(params, w, thetas)
            test_iters.append(i)
            accs_test.append(acc_test)

        rng = random.fold_in(key, i)
        params_grad, w_grad, thetas_grad = private_grad(params, w, thetas[ids],
                X, y, rng, C, noise_mult, L)

        if steptheta > 0:
            thetas[ids] -= L * steptheta * thetas_grad   # local updates

        if stepw > 0:
            w -= stepw * w_grad                          # global head update
            params = update_params(params, params_grad, stepw)  # CNN params update
            priv.step(noise_multiplier=noise_mult, sample_rate=L/ds.N)

        eps, best_alpha = priv.get_privacy_spent(delta=delta)
        epss.append(eps)

    return {'test_iters': test_iters, 'accs_test': accs_test, 'epss': epss}

# ===== Alpha-to-steps mapping used in all runners (main_real.py, main_nn.py, main_synthetic.py) =====
# Given base step, and personalization factor alpha:
# - If 0 <= alpha <= 1:     step_w = alpha * step; step_theta = step
# - If alpha == -1 (inf):   step_w = step;         step_theta = 0       (global-only)
# - If alpha >= 1:          step_w = step;         step_theta = step/alpha

def map_alpha_to_steps(step, alpha):
    if 0.0 <= alpha <= 1.0:
        return alpha * step, step
    if alpha == -1:
        return step, 0.0
    if alpha >= 1.0:
        return step, step / alpha
    raise ValueError('Unsupported alpha')
llm_extracted_info.experimental_info:
- Objective and model
  - Stochastic objective over global w and per-user local parameters theta_i with additive model: minimize E[в„“(y, (w + theta_i)^T x)].
  - Implemented with squared loss (regression on one-hot labels): y is centered via y -= 0.1 before gradient computation.

- PPSGD (billboard JDP) update per round
  - For the selected users ids (|ids| = L):
    1) Local gradient for each user i: g_i^theta = E_b[вҲҮ_theta в„“(y, (w + theta_i)^T x)] (estimated on user mini-batch).
    2) Local update (no privacy): theta_i вҶҗ theta_i вҲ’ О·_theta g_i^theta.
    3) Clip each userвҖҷs contribution by L2 norm C: g_i^clip = g_i^theta / max(1, ||g_i^theta||_2 / C).
    4) Server aggregates global gradient: g_w = (1/L) ОЈ_i g_i^clip, adds Gaussian noise О¶ ~ N(0, (Пғ C / L)^2 I), and updates w вҶҗ w вҲ’ О·_w (g_w + О¶).
  - Privacy accountant: Opacus RDPAccountant with sampling rate q = L / N; noise multiplier set by noise_mult (Пғ). Track Оө at given Оҙ each round.

- Client sampling and minibatching
  - Each round samples L = clients_per_round users without replacement from N users who still have data; per-user gradients are computed on a batch of size b from the userвҖҷs data stream.
  - Sample rate for privacy accounting per round: q = L / N.

- Alpha-dependent geometry (step-size mapping)
  - Given base step parameter step and personalization parameter alpha, steps are scheduled as:
    - 0 вүӨ alpha вүӨ 1: step_w = alpha * step, step_theta = step.
    - alpha = вҲ’1 (вҲһ): step_w = step, step_theta = 0 (global-only training).
    - alpha вүҘ 1: step_w = step, step_theta = step / alpha.

- Noise and clipping
  - Per-user gradient contribution for w is clipped to norm C.
  - Aggregated global gradient is noised with isotropic Gaussian noise with std Пғ_О¶ = noise_mult * C / L per coordinate (real/synthetic linear code) or l2_norm_clip * noise_mult / num_users (CNN/JAX variant).

- Datasets and batching
  - EMNIST (tff.simulation.datasets.emnist):
    - d = 784, n_classes = 10; default N = 3383.
    - batches(): each round selects L clients, each contributes one batch of size b; number of epochs configurable.
  - StackOverflow (preprocessed pickles expected):
    - d = input feature dimension, n_classes = tag vocabulary size; default N = 500.
    - batches(): identical schedule as EMNIST; labels are multi-class indicators and accuracy counts hits on predicted argmax.
  - Synthetic dataset:
    - True per-user parameters theta0_i = mu0 + local_weight * Оҫ masked to a subset of features.
    - Feature variances var_j = 1 / (1 + j), label noise Пғ = 0.01.

- Default hyperparameters (CLI defaults)
  - clients_per_round (L): 10
  - N (users): EMNIST: 3383; StackOverflow: 500
  - batch size (b): 10
  - num_epochs: 1
  - eval_delta: 500 (evaluate test accuracy and report Оө every eval_delta rounds)
  - delta (DP): 1e-4
  - step (base): dataset-dependent grids below.
  - noise_mult (Пғ): dataset-dependent grids below.
  - clipping C: dataset-dependent grids below.

- Hyperparameter grids (real data, main_real.py)
  - EMNIST:
    - step вҲҲ {1e-4, 2e-4, 5e-4, 1e-3, 2e-3, 5e-3, 1e-2}
    - alpha вҲҲ {0, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100, вҲ’1}
    - noise_mult вҲҲ {2, 5, 20, 50, 70}
    - C вҲҲ {1, 10, 100}
  - StackOverflow:
    - step вҲҲ {2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250}
    - alpha вҲҲ {0, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100, вҲ’1}
    - noise_mult вҲҲ {0.02, 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, 2, 5}
    - C вҲҲ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10}

- Hyperparameter grids (CNN/JAX, main_nn.py)
  - EMNIST:
    - step вҲҲ {0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5}
    - alpha вҲҲ {0, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100, вҲ’1}
    - noise_mult вҲҲ {0, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100}
    - C вҲҲ {0.1, 1, 10}
  - StackOverflow:
    - step вҲҲ {2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250}
    - alpha вҲҲ {0, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100, вҲ’1}
    - noise_mult вҲҲ {0.02, 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, 2, 5}
    - C вҲҲ {0.01}

- Hyperparameter grids (synthetic, main_synthetic.py)
  - step вҲҲ {0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.7, 1.0, 1.2, 1.5, 1.8}
  - alpha вҲҲ {0, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100, вҲ’1}
  - noise_mult вҲҲ {0, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100, 300, 1000}
  - C вҲҲ {0.1, 1, 10, 100}

- Metrics and logging
  - Real/CNN: track test accuracy vs iterations; compute and store Оө after each round with delta = 1e-4.
  - Synthetic: track excess risk E[||w+theta_i вҲ’ theta0_i||^2_ОЈ] and Оө vs iteration.

- Notes
  - Noise scale in code corresponds to Пғ_О¶ вүҘ cВ·C / (L) up to the experimental multiplier noise_mult; Оө,Оҙ-privacy calibration can be tightened by setting noise_mult according to desired Оө using the RDP accountant.
  - The JAX CNN variant adds DP noise to both the global linear head and the shared CNN parameters, using the same per-user clipping and aggregation pattern; local user-specific head theta_i remains private and is updated locally without noise.

research_study_list.title: Personalization Improves Privacy-Accuracy Tradeoffs in Federated Learning
research_study_list.abstract:
Large-scale machine learning systems often involve data distributed across a
collection of users. Federated learning algorithms leverage this structure by
communicating model updates to a central server, rather than entire datasets.
In this paper, we study stochastic optimization algorithms for a personalized
federated learning setting involving local and global models subject to
user-level (joint) differential privacy. While learning a private global model
induces a cost of privacy, local learning is perfectly private. We provide
generalization guarantees showing that coordinating local learning with private
centralized learning yields a generically useful and improved tradeoff between
accuracy and privacy. We illustrate our theoretical results with experiments on
synthetic and real-world datasets.
research_study_list.full_text: Personalization Improves PrivacyвҖ“Accuracy Tradeoffs in Federated Learning Alberto Bietti 1 Chen-Yu Wei2 Miroslav DudВҙДұk3 John Langford 3 Zhiwei Steven Wu4 Abstract Large-scale machine learning systems often in- volve data distributed across a collection of users. Federated learning algorithms leverage this struc- ture by communicating model updates to a central server, rather than entire datasets. In this paper, we study stochastic optimization algorithms for a personalized federated learning setting involv- ing local and global models subject to user-level (joint) differential privacy. While learning a pri- vate global model induces a cost of privacy, local learning is perfectly private. We provide gener- alization guarantees showing that coordinating local learning with private centralized learning yields a generically useful and improved tradeoff between accuracy and privacy. We illustrate our theoretical results with experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets. 1. Introduction Many modern applications of machine learning involve data from a large set of users. In such settings, both privacy considerations and bandwidth limits may require keeping each userвҖҷs data on their device, instead communicating with a centralized server via shared model updates, a scenario commonly known as federated learning (Kairouz et al., 2021). When the data distribution varies across users, it is often beneп¬Ғcial to consider personalized models where parts of the model ( e.g., user-speciп¬Ғc embeddings, or additive offsets) are local to each user, leading to updates that may be performed locally. Such personalized federated learning has been deployed at scale in a wide range of ap- plications, including keyboard next-word prediction (Wang et al., 2019), automated speech recognition, and news 1Center for Data Science, New York University2University of Southern California 3Microsoft Research, New York 4Carnegie Mellon University. Correspondence to: Alberto Bietti <al- berto.bietti@nyu.edu>. Proceedings of the 39 th International Conference on Machine Learning, Baltimore, Maryland, USA, PMLR 162, 2022. Copy- right 2022 by the author(s). personalization (Paulik et al., 2021). In this paper, we focus on personalized federated learning algorithms based on stochastic optimization, which are the most widely used in this context (McMahan et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2021). Concretely, we consider stochastic opti- mization problems of the following form: min w,Оё1:N { f(w,Оё1:N) := 1 N NвҲ‘ i=1 EОҫвҲјPi[fi(w,Оёi,Оҫ)] } , (1) where N is the number of users, w a global parameter, Оё1:N = (Оё1,...,Оё N) a set of local parameters, and Pi de- notes the sample distribution for user i. Similar objectives have been considered in other works (Agarwal et al., 2020; Hanzely et al., 2021). The personalization problem raises the question of how much learning should happen at the local vs global level: if the local models are expressive enough, local learning alone should sufп¬Ғce to learn good models with enough samples per user, yet if the data has some shared structure across users, a global model may help us learn more efп¬Ғciently. Although federated learning algorithms do not store user data in the central server, private user information may still be leaked in the resulting models, unless appropriate mech- anisms are applied to guarantee privacy. In this work, we consider the notion of user-level (joint) differential privacy (DP) (Dwork et al., 2006; 2010a; Kearns et al., 2014), which ensures an adversary cannot reliably detect the presence or absence of all the data associated with a single user based on the output information. Such a notion has been used successfully in federated learning problems, with practical optimization algorithms that lead to useful privacy guaran- tees (McMahan et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2021). Unfortunately, assuring such privacy may come at a cost to accuracy (see, for example, the lower bounds of Bassily et al., 2014). The key question we address is: Can we leverage personalization to improve privacyвҖ“ accuracy tradeoffs in federated learning? The insight motivating our work is that in personalized fed- erated learning, it is only the global portion of the optimiza- tion that needs to experience this drop in accuracy. A userвҖҷs arXiv:2202.05318v2  [stat.ML]  15 Jul 2022Personalization Improves PrivacyвҖ“Accuracy Tradeoffs in Federated Learning local model can compensate for the privacy-guaranteeing accuracy limitations of the global model. We formalize this by considering algorithms with a personalization parame- ter Оұthat may vary the level of personalization from local learning (Оұ= 0) to global learning (Оұ= вҲһ). We then show generalization bounds on the objective 1 of the form f(wn,Оё1:N,n)вҲ’fвҲ—вүӨCstat(Оұ,n,N )+ Cpriv(Оұ,Пө,N ), (2) where fвҲ—= minw,Оё1:N f(w,Оё1:N) is the optimal risk, nis the number of observed samples per user and Пөis the DP privacy parameter. Here, we expect the second term to vanish when ОұвҶ’0, as local learning does not suffer from privacy. Crucially, this privacy cost does not depend on the number of samples per user n, while the п¬Ғrst term, which captures statistical efп¬Ғciency, generally decreases with n for any Оұ. This emphasizes how adjusting the level of personalization through Оұcan help improve generalization by adjusting the trade-off between these two terms. Concretely, we provide precise guarantees of this form for simple federated private stochastic gradient algorithms, where the number of iterations corresponds to the number of samples per user n, and the personalization parameter Оұ is given by the relative step-size between global and local updates. In particular, we show thatОұaffects the complexity of learning by changing the geometry of the optimization: in problems that beneп¬Ғt from global models, small Оұmakes learning more difп¬Ғcult but reduces the cost of privacy. We complement our theoretical results with experiments on synthetic and real-world federated learning datasets, which illustrate how varying the step-size ratio leads to improved trade-offs between accuracy and privacy. 2. Related Work Model personalizaton. There are a variety of approaches for personalization in federated learning. In local п¬Ғne- tuning, a global model is learnt by federated learning and then used as a warm start for on-device learning from the cache of local data (Wang et al., 2019; Paulik et al., 2021). This approach can be augmented with federated learning of hyperparameters (Wang et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2019; Khodak et al., 2019) to obtain federated learning variants of meta-learning approaches like MAML (Finn et al., 2017) and Reptile (Nichol et al., 2018). Another approach is to view personalization as a multi-task learning problem, and learn task-speciп¬Ғc models with a regularization that forces parameters for similar tasks to be close, e.g., in a Euclidean norm (Vanhaesebrouck et al., 2017; Smith et al., 2017; Arivazhagan et al., 2019; Mansour et al., 2020; Dinh et al., 2020; Shen et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2021; Marfoq et al., 2021; Singhal et al., 2021). Task simi- larity is typically expressed as a weighted undirected graph, a matrix, or a clustering. A special case of the multi-task ap- proach is to learn a global model in addition to local models, which are regularized to be close to the global model (Man- sour et al., 2020; Deng et al., 2020; Hanzely & Richt Вҙarik, 2020; Hanzely et al., 2021; Marfoq et al., 2021). This is closest to the approach here, which also separates global and local parameters. However, the approach here is more general, because we allow a broader range of modeling rela- tionships between the global and local parameters, similar to federated residual learning (Agarwal et al., 2020). The sta- tistical aspects of such personalization models were studied by Mansour et al. (2020); Agarwal et al. (2020), who in par- ticular provide generalization guarantees for additive person- alization models similar to ours. However, compared to the present paper, these works do not provide privacy guarantees nor study the effect of varying the level of personalization. Privacy. The results here advance a recent line of work that provides formal user-level privacy guarantees for model personalizaton in federated learning. Similar to the prior work of Jain et al. (2021) and Hu et al. (2021), we adopt the privacy formulation of joint differential privacy (JDP, Kearns et al., 2014), a variation of DP that is more suitable for the problem of model personalization than standard DP. Jain et al. (2021) provides private algorithms that п¬Ғrst learn a shared linear representation for all users, and allow each user to learn their local linear regression model over the lower-dimensional representation. This leads to a factorized model of personalization, which is different than ours and not handled by our theoretical assumptions due to non-convexity. Hu et al. (2021) provides a private personalizaton algorithm through the mean-regularized multi-task learning objective without establishing its statistical rates. In contrast, we consider more general personalization schemes and provide statistical guarantees. The results here are also related to other work in DP with a similar motivation to model personalization. Li et al. (2020) studies meta-learning under DP. Their framework does not cover model personalization with a separate model for each user. Noble et al. (2021) studies federated learning with DP with heterogeneous data across nodes, but they do not support personalization. Bellet et al. (2018) studies fully decentralized DP algorithms for collaborative learning over a network instead of federated learning. Finally, the notion of user-level privacy has also been adopted in prior works (McMahan et al., 2018; Levy et al., 2021), but these do not consider model personalization. 3. Preliminaries In this section, we introduce the problem of personalized federated optimization, as well as the notion of user-level privacy that we consider.Personalization Improves PrivacyвҖ“Accuracy Tradeoffs in Federated Learning 3.1. Problem setting We consider stochastic optimization problems of the form min w,Оё1:N { f(w,Оё1:N) := 1 N NвҲ‘ i=1 fi(w,Оёi) } , where N is the number of users, w вҲҲRdw a global pa- rameter, Оё1:N = ( Оё1,...,Оё N) вҲҲ (RdОё)N a set of local parameters, and fi(w,Оёi) := EОҫвҲјPi[fi(w,Оёi,Оҫ)] is the ex- pected risk of user i, with random samples Оҫdrawn from an unknown user-speciп¬Ғc distribution Pi. While our algorithms may be run on arbitrary differen- tiable models, our analysis focuses on the convex setting, where fi(w,Оёi,Оҫi) is jointly convex in (w,Оёi) for all Оҫi. Additive model. An important special case is the additive model for supervised learning, where dw = dОё = d, and fi(w,Оёi,(x,y)) = в„“(y,(w+ Оёi)вҠӨx), (3) where в„“is a loss function andОҫ= (x,y) is a training sample. As a running example, consider a movie recommendation app, which seeks to predict how each user iwill rate any given movie. In this case, x corresponds to the features describing a movie (e.g., its genre, popularity, length, actors, etc.), and yis the userвҖҷs rating of that movie. Following the federated protocol, the data about user activity stays on the device and only model parameters can be communicated to the server. In this case, only the information about the global parameter wis communicated. The additive model makes the optimization problem (1) underdetermined, with many possible equivalent solutions obtained by adding any vector v вҲҲRd to wand then sub- tracting v from each Оёi, effectively вҖңshiftingвҖқ the predic- tive ability between global and local parameters. This is what allows the optimization algorithm to achieve different tradeoffs between statistical generalization (accuracy) and sharing of information across users (privacy). To develop the intuition about this tradeoff, п¬Ғrst consider the homogeneous scenario, where the optimal parame- ters ОёвҲ— i вҲҲarg minОёE(x,y)вҲјPi[в„“(y,ОёвҠӨx)] for each user are equal (ОёвҲ— 1 = В·В·В· = ОёвҲ— N). There are two extreme approaches: (i) local learning, where only Оёi are trained individually for each user, leading to poor sample complexity but per- fect privacy (ii) global learning, where only wis trained, and we beneп¬Ғt from using samples from all users, but need communication with a centralized server and lose privacy. In the more realistic heterogeneous scenario, when the ОёвҲ— i are different but have some shared components, e.g., only some coordinates differ across users, joint learning of w and Оё1:N can achieve greater accuracy (at the same number of samples) than both local and global learning, by leverag- ing more samples to estimate the shared components, while beneп¬Ғting from user-speciп¬Ғc data to personalize. Note that in this case, it is possible to further improve privacy, by al- lowing some shared components to be п¬Ғtted entirely locally. Quantifying this improvement is the focus of our work. In the movie recommendation example, if only global learn- ing is performed, the prediction wвҠӨxcan use overall popu- larity statistics, but the resulting prediction is the same for all users, without any personalization. On the other hand, if only local learning is performed, although the system can fully personalize and provide full privacy, the quality of rec- ommendation is limited by the number of movies the user watched before. With joint learning, the system can cap- ture both global trends through wand adapt to each userвҖҷs preference through Оёi. 3.2. User-level (joint) differential privacy We aim to provide user-level privacy (Dwork et al., 2010a), which ensures that an adverary cannot detect the presence or absence of all of the data associated with a single user when given the output of the algorithm. To achieve this goal, we design algorithms using the billboard model (Hsu et al., 2014) ofdifferential privacy(DP) (Dwork et al., 2006). Let us п¬Ғrst revisit the deп¬Ғnition of DP, which informally requires that changing any single userвҖҷs data cannot change the algorithmвҖҷs output by much. Deп¬Ғnition 3.1 (User-level DP; Dwork et al., 2006; 2010a). A randomized mechanism M is (Пө,Оҙ)-differential privacy (DP) if for all pairs of data sets D,DвҖІthat differ by a single userвҖҷs data and all eventsEin the output range, Pr[M(D) вҲҲE] вүӨeПөPr[M(DвҖІ) вҲҲE] + Оҙ. Billboard model. In the billboard model, a server com- putes aggregate information subject to the constraint of DP and shares the information as public messages with all N users. Then, based on the public messages and their own private data, each user computes their own personalized model. The billboard model is particularly compatible with algorithms in the federated setting, where the DP messages are typically noisy summary statistics about the usersвҖҷ lo- cal models (Jain et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2021). Hsu et al. (2014) show that algorithms under the billboard model pro- vide an extremely strong privacy guarantee, known as joint differential privacy (JDP, Kearns et al., 2014). Joint differential privacy (JDP). Let Di denote the col- lection of samples associated with each user i. Two datasets Dand DвҖІare called i-neighbors if they only differ by user iвҖҷs private data. For any mechanismM, we denote MвҲ’i(D) as the output information to all other users except user i.Personalization Improves PrivacyвҖ“Accuracy Tradeoffs in Federated Learning Algorithm 1 Personalized-Private-SGD (PPSGD) 1: Input: О·: step-size, Оұ: global/local ratio, ПғО¶: privacy noise level, C: clipping parameter. 2: Initialize w0 = Оё0 = 0. 3: for t= 1 to ndo 4: for all clients iin parallel do 5: Sample data Оҫi,t вҲјPi 6: Compute gt Оё,i = вҲҮОёfi(wtвҲ’1,Оёi,tвҲ’1,Оҫi,t) gt w,i = вҲҮwfi(wtвҲ’1,Оёi,tвҲ’1,Оҫi,t) 7: Update Оёi,t = Оёi,tвҲ’1 вҲ’О· Ngt Оё,i 8: Clip gradient: Лңgt w,i = gt w,i/max(1, вҲҘgt w,iвҲҘ C ) 9: Send Лңgt w,i to the server 10: end for 11: Sample О¶t вҲјN(0,Пғ2 О¶Idw) 12: Update wt = wtвҲ’1 вҲ’ОұО·( 1 N вҲ‘N i=1 Лңgt w,i + О¶t) 13: end for Deп¬Ғnition 3.2 (Joint-Differential Privacy, JDP). An algo- rithm M is (Пө,Оҙ)-jointly differentially private, written as (Пө,Оҙ)-JDP, if for all i, all i-neighboring datasets D,DвҖІ, and all events Ein the output space to all other users except i, Pr[MвҲ’i(D) вҲҲE] вүӨeПөPr[MвҲ’i(DвҖІ) вҲҲE] + Оҙ. In the setting of model personalization, JDP implies that even if all of users except i collude, potentially pooling their private data and local models together, user iвҖҷs private data are still protected, so long as idoes not reveal their own model. 4. Main Algorithm and Analysis Our main algorithm, shown in Algorithm 1, is a personalized version of distributed SGD (Dekel et al., 2012), with a per- sonalization parameter Оұthat controls the relative step-size between global and local updates. At each round t, each user isamples a fresh datapoint Оҫi,t (this could also be a mini-batch), updates its local model Оёi, and sends the gradient with respect towto the central server, which aggregates gradients of all users before updating the global model w. The total number of rounds nthus corre- sponds to the number of samples per user. In order to guar- antee user-level privacy, we clip each userвҖҷs gradientsgt w,i before aggregation, and add Gaussian noise, following com- mon practice (Abadi et al., 2016; McMahan et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2020). The choice of Оұaffects the degree by which the algorithm favors local learning over global learning, with Оұ= 0 forcing local-only updates. Privacy analysis. We п¬Ғrst establish the formal user-level JDP guarantee of Algorithm 1. Theorem 4.1 (Privacy). Suppose we set the noise parameter ПғО¶ вүҘcC вҲҡ nlog(1/Оҙ) NПө , (4) for an absolute constant c, then Algorithm 1 satisп¬Ғes (Пө,Оҙ)- JDP in the billboard model. We defer the full proof to Appendix B.1. At a high level, the proof п¬Ғrst shows that the aggregate information released by the server (the sequence of global model updates) satisп¬Ғes (Пө,Оҙ)-DP. Since the sequence of global models are sufп¬Ғcient statistics for each user to identify their personalized model Оёi, the JDP guarantee follows from the billboard lemma (see Lemma B.1). Eq.(10) suggests that this threshold on the user sample size nscales quadrati- cally with the number of users N, linearly with the privacy level Пө, and inversely with the dimension dw. When the optimal user parameters are different, this transition would likely happen for smaller n, as we expect local models to be useful even at small sample sizes regardless of privacy. Improving the bias term. We remark that the bias term decreases as 1/nin both local and global learning scenarios described above, and does not improve with the number of users. As in standard SGD ( e.g., Dekel et al., 2012), this term decreases with the number of rounds, and may thus decrease more quickly with the number of samples if more communication rounds are performed for the same total number of samples. In Section 5, we show that sampling users at each round can help improve this term. Comparison to Local SGD. A common approach for fed- erated optimization is the local SGD (or federated averaging) algorithm (McMahan et al., 2017), which performs multiple local steps before communicating with the server. This is in contrast to our method, which more closely resembles mini-batch SGD. We note that despite its practical success, the known theoretical guarantees of local SGD typically do not improve on mini-batch SGD except for speciп¬Ғc set- tings (Woodworth et al., 2020). Our study therefore focuses on understanding personalization in the SGD setting, but we note that extending our analysis to local SGD is an inter- esting direction for future work. 5. Experiments In this section, we present numerical experiments that il- lustrate our theoretical guarantees on both synthetic and real-world federated learning datasets. Our code is available at https://github.com/albietz/ppsgd. Experiment setup. We consider linear additive models of the form (3) with the squared loss. For classiп¬Ғcation datasets, we use a one-versus-all setup. Unless otherwise mentioned, we run our algorithms for one pass over the data, simulating the online setting considered by our the- ory. We sample a п¬Ғxed number of users (denoted Q) at each iteration, chosen randomly without replacement, and consider minibatches of size m= 10 for each user. Follow- ing standard practice, we compute privacy guarantees Пөat each iteration using the moments accountant (Abadi et al., 2016), with a п¬Ғxed Оҙ= 10вҲ’4, after applying Gaussian noise of variance Пғ2 О¶ = Пғ2C2, where C is a clipping parame- ter and Пғ a noise multiplier. For each run, we consider a п¬Ғxed step-size О·, personalization parameter Оұ, clipping parameter C, and noise multiplier Пғ, chosen from a grid (see Appendix A). In order to assess the best achievable performance, we optimize the learning rate separately at each number of iterations reported in our п¬Ғgures. Synthetic dataset. We begin with a synthetic regression example, where each userвҖҷs data is generated from a ground truth linear model, and multiple coordinates of the parame- ters are shared across users. We consider N = 1000 users and data in d = 100 dimensions. The red, blue, and light green curves are obtained by varying the amount of privacy noise Пғ while optimizing the learning rate with local, global, and intermediate models, respectively. The black curve also optimizes over the level of personalization, which improves the tradeoff. In Figure 1b, we show the top-1 accuracy on the test set, while running our method for a single pass over the data, sampling Q = 10 users and m = 10 documents per user at each iteration. We observe that without privacy, the best performance is obtained with an intermediate amount of personalization, Оұ= 10, suggesting that local models are helpful for generalization on this dataset. With privacy noise, however, models with large Оұ quickly degrade in performance, and small Оұperforms best for achieving rea- sonable privacy levels. Overall, personalization plays a key role for improving the privacyвҖ“accuracy tradeoff on this dataset, as shown in Figure 2. Federated EMNIST. Finally, we consider a subset of the federated EMNIST digit classiп¬Ғcation dataset2 consisting of N = 1000 users with about 100 training and 10 test samples per user. Here, a single pass does not lead to good performance, suggesting this may be a hard learning prob- lem that beneп¬Ғts from multiple passes (Pillaud-Vivien et al., 2018). We thus run our method for 20 epochs, sampling 10 users and 10 images per user at each iteration, and show the resulting test accuracy in Figure 1c. On this dataset, we see that local learning does generally poorly, perhaps because there is a lot of shared structure in images across users that beneп¬Ғts signiп¬Ғcantly from global learning. Never- theless, there is still a beneп¬Ғt to using small Оұfor achieving good privacy guarantees, and personalization still plays a role in improving the privacyвҖ“accuracy tradeoff (albeit less pronounced than on Stackoverп¬Ӯow, see Figure 2). In Figure 1d, we show similar plots on federated EM- NIST for a 4-layer CNN with a shared global represen- tation ОҰW(x) and an additive model at the output layer, leading to a loss of the form fi((w,W ),Оёi,(x,y)) = в„“(y,(w+ Оёi)вҠӨОҰW(x)). We see that similar conclusions hold in this non-convex 2https://www.tensorflow.org/federated/ api_docs/python/tff/simulation/datasets/ emnist/load_data scenario, namely, small Оұ is helpful for achieving better privacy guarantees, while large Оұleads to better accuracy for poor privacy levels. Here the local-only models (Оұ= 0) perform very poorly, since the global representation has frozen random weights, highlighting that learning a useful representation in this model requires a privacy cost. 7. Discussion and Conclusion In this paper, we studied personalized private federated opti- mization algorithms, providing generalization guarantees in the convex setting that highlight the role of personalization and privacy. We show both theoretically and empirically that adjusting the amount of personalization is always beneп¬Ғcial for improving the accuracyвҖ“privacy tradeoff, by providing a way to attenuate the cost of privacy through local learning at large sample sizes. Promising future directions include extending our analysis to different models of personaliza- tion, e.g., based on neural networks, and to provide adaptive algorithms which may dynamically adjust the level of per- sonalization in an online fashion. Suppose a message broadcasting mechanism M: DвҶ’W is (Пө,Оҙ)- differentially private. Consider any set of functions: gi : DiГ—WвҶ’W вҖІ. To prove the JDP guarantee, by the billboard lemma (Lemma B.1), it is sufп¬Ғcient to show that the global model updates satisfy standard DP w.r.t. any changes to a single userвҖҷs data. With the assumption that gradients are uniformly bounded byG, each update of the global models has в„“2 sensitivity bounded by ОұО·/N w.r.t. any change to a single userвҖҷs data. If we take ПғО¶ вүҘcG вҲҡ nlog(1/Оҙ) NПө , for some absolute constant c, then by the Gaussian mechanism, each update is ( ПөвҲҡ nlog(1/Оҙ) ,Оҙ)-differentially private, and by the advanced composition theorem (Dwork et al., 2010b), the algorithm that outputs global models is (Пө,Оҙ)-differentially private after nsteps.Personalization Improves PrivacyвҖ“Accuracy Tradeoffs in Federated Learning When considering both local and global models as outputs of the algorithm, combining the above with the billboard lemma yields the desired JDP guarantee.
