Optimizing average user risk with heterogeneous sample sizes In this section, we study a variant of Algorithm 2 which optimizes the average risk f instead of the weighted risk fm in (11), as described in Section 5. The algorithm is described in Algorithm 3, and we provide its generalization and privacy guarantees below. Theorem B.4 (Generalization and privacy for Algorithm 3). Under Assumptions 4.2, 4.3, and 4.4, let zвҲ—= (wвҲ—,Оё1:N вҲ—) be any minimizer of f, LОұ = Lmax { Оұ+ Оұ N, 1 N } , and Пғ2 m,Оұ := ОұВҜПғ2 w,m + ОұЛңПғ2 w,m + ВҜПғ2 Оё,m qN + ОұdwПғ2 О¶ вүӨОұВҜПғ2 w + ВҜПғ2 Оё qNmmin + ОұЛңПғ2 w,m qN + ОұdwПғ2 О¶, (35) where ВҜПғ2 w,m := 1 N вҲ‘ i Пғ2 w,i mi and ВҜПғ2 Оё,m := 1 N вҲ‘ i Пғ2 Оё,i mi and ЛңПғ2 w,m := 1 N вҲ‘ iq(1 вҲ’q)вҲҘвҲҮwfi(wвҲ—,ОёвҲ— i)вҲҘ2. With О·= min { 1 4LОұ , вҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘОұвҲҡ TПғm,Оұ } and C = G, Algorithm 3 satisп¬Ғes E[f(ВҜzT) вҲ’f(zвҲ—)] вүӨ4LОұвҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘ2 Оұ T + 3Пғm,ОұвҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘОұвҲҡ T , with ВҜzT = 1 T вҲ‘TвҲ’1 t=0 zt. For c1,c2 as in Theorem 5.1, and for any Пө<c 1q2T, if we take ПғО¶ = c2 C вҲҡ Tlog(1/Оҙ) NПө , then Algorithm 3 is (Пө,Оҙ)-JDP , and the generalization guarantee becomes: E[f(ВҜzT) вҲ’f(zвҲ—)] вүІ LОұвҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘ2 Оұ T + вҲҘzвҲ—вҲҘОұ вҲҡ ОұВҜПғ2w,m + ОұЛңПғ2w,m + ВҜПғ2 Оё,m qNT + ОұdwG2 log(1 Оҙ) N2Пө2 . (36) Proof. The proof can be obtained by replacing fi by Лңfi = M miNfi in the proofs of Theorem 5.2 and Theorem 5.1.

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Addresses how to balance accuracy and user-level privacy in personalized federated learning by separating global and local model components. Introduces Personalized-Private-SGD (PPSGD) with a personalization parameter Оұ controlling the relative emphasis on global (private) vs local (perfectly private) learning. Derives generalization bounds that decompose excess risk into a statistical term (improves with per-user samples n) and a privacy term (independent of n, scales with Оұ, dimension, and privacy parameters), formalizing that personalization can mitigate the privacy cost. Proves user-level Joint Differential Privacy (JDP) guarantees via the billboard model with Gaussian noise. Extends to client sampling and heterogeneous minibatches with privacy amplification and corresponding convergence bounds. Empirically demonstrates improved privacyвҖ“accuracy tradeoffs on synthetic and real-world datasets by tuning Оұ.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: Formulates a stochastic optimization objective with global parameter w and per-user parameters Оё1:N, including an additive model fi(w,Оёi,(x,y))=в„“(y,(w+Оёi)^Tx). Proposes PPSGD: per round, each user computes gradients wrt Оёi and w on local data; updates Оёi locally; clips gt_w,i by norm C; server aggregates clipped gt_w,i, adds Gaussian noise О¶~N(0,ПғО¶^2 I), and updates w with step size ОұО·. Оұ tunes personalization (Оұ=0 local-only; ОұвҶ’вҲһ global-only). Privacy: calibrates ПғО¶ using Gaussian mechanism; shows DP for global updates and JDP for full personalization via the billboard lemma. Generalization: under convexity, L-smoothness, and bounded gradients, analyzes convergence using an Оұ-weighted norm; obtains bounds with bias O(1/n) and variance O(1/вҲҡn), with privacy cost independent of n. Extends to client sampling (prob q) and heterogeneous minibatches mi, yielding additional variance terms and privacy amplification; also provides a variant optimizing average user risk (Algorithm 3). Provides a threshold condition on n where increasing personalization (smaller Оұ) improves generalization under privacy.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: Implements PPSGD with moments accountant for privacy (Оҙ=1e-4) and Gaussian noise ПғО¶^2=Пғ^2 C^2; tunes learning rate per iteration from grids; clips gradients with dataset-specific C; samples Q users per iteration with minibatch size m=10 unless stated. Synthetic regression: N=1000, d=100; Оё*i share first 95 dims, last 5 personalized; x~N(0,diag(1/k)), y=Оё*i^T x+Оө; one pass, Q=N, m=10, C=10; reports excess risk (closed form). StackOverflow tag prediction (TFF): N=500 users, 300вҖ“500 train and 10вҖ“100 test docs/user, bag-of-words dim 5000; 80 labels (one-vs-all); one pass with Q=10, m=10, C=0.01; reports top-1 test accuracy and Оө over iterations. Federated EMNIST: N=1000 users (~100 train, 10 test/user). Linear model (one-vs-all): 20 passes, Q=10, m=10, C=10. CNN (4-layer shared representation with additive head): same schedule, C=0.1. Plots accuracy/excess risk vs iterations for multiple Оұ under fixed Пғ and accuracyвҖ“privacy (Оө) tradeoff curves by varying Пғ and Оұ.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: Theoretical guarantees assume convex, L-smooth losses and bounded gradients; non-convex settings (e.g., CNN) are only evaluated empirically. Analysis presumes fresh i.i.d. samples per round and synchronous participation. Privacy guarantees target user-level JDP via the billboard model and require users not to reveal their local models. Gradient clipping is idealized (often set C=G) and large G can weaken privacy; clipping effects on optimization are not analyzed. Privacy cost grows with dimension and can dominate in high-d settings. Heterogeneous minibatch sizes increase privacy cost (scales with mmax/M). Bias term decays as 1/n and does not improve with more users unless changing sampling/rounds. Does not analyze Local SGD/FedAvg theoretically. Choice of Оұ is not adaptive and selection requires tuning. Empirical validation spans a limited set of datasets and models.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: Extend theory to non-convex models and neural personalization (e.g., shared representations, meta-learning). Develop adaptive schemes to tune Оұ online based on progress, privacy budget, or per-user data characteristics. Theoretically analyze Local SGD/FedAvg and multi-round communication under JDP. Study clipping adaptivity and gradient-norm estimation to reduce privacy noise. Explore alternative personalization structures (graph/clustering/matrix regularization, factorized representations) with privacy guarantees. Design personalized privacy budgets (per-user Оө) and fairness-aware personalization. Improve bounds to reduce dimensional dependence and tighten constants; investigate stronger privacy amplification strategies. Address system heterogeneity, partial participation, and integration with secure aggregation. Broaden empirical evaluation to more tasks, larger models, and cross-silo settings.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_code:
# ===== Extracted code implementing PPSGD (per-user personalization, clipped-and-noised global updates) =====

# -----------------------------
# From main_synthetic.py (additive linear model with squared loss)
# -----------------------------
from opacus.accountants.rdp import RDPAccountant
import numpy as np

def training_priv(ds, batch_size=10,
                  step=0.5, stepw=None, steptheta=None,
                  niter=300, C=10., noise_mult=0.05, sample_rate=1., delta=1e-4):
    w = np.zeros(ds.d)
    thetas = np.zeros((ds.N, ds.d))

    priv = RDPAccountant()

    if stepw is None:
        stepw = step
    if steptheta is None:
        steptheta = step

    ls = []
    epss = []
    for i in range(niter):
        X, y = ds.get_batch(batch_size)
        pred = np.sum(X * (w[None,:] + thetas), axis=2)

        thetas_grad = np.sum(X * (pred - y)[:,:,None], axis=0) / batch_size
        if steptheta > 0:
            thetas -= steptheta * thetas_grad

        thetas_grad /= np.clip(np.linalg.norm(thetas_grad, axis=1) / C, a_min=1., a_max=None)[:,None]
        w_grad = np.mean(thetas_grad, axis=0)
        if stepw > 0:
            w -= stepw * (w_grad + noise_mult * C * np.random.randn(ds.d) / ds.N)

            priv.step(noise_multiplier=noise_mult, sample_rate=1.)

        ls.append(ds.excess_risk(w, thetas))
        eps, best_alpha = priv.get_privacy_spent(delta=delta)
        epss.append(eps)
    return ls, epss


# -----------------------------
# From main_real.py (real federated datasets; linear additive model)
# -----------------------------
from opacus.accountants.rdp import RDPAccountant
import numpy as np
import time

def training_priv(ds, step=0.5, stepw=None, steptheta=None,
                  C=10., noise_mult=0.05, delta=1e-4, eval_delta=500):
    w = np.zeros((ds.d, ds.n_classes))
    thetas = np.zeros((ds.N, ds.d, ds.n_classes))

    priv = RDPAccountant()

    if stepw is None:
        stepw = step
    if steptheta is None:
        steptheta = step

    ls = []
    accs = []
    accs_test = []
    test_iters = []
    epss = []

    for i, (ids, X, y) in enumerate(ds.batches()):
        L = len(ids)
        X = X[:,:L,:]  # [B,N,d]
        y = y[:,:L,:]  # [B,N,K]
        y -= 0.1
        pred = np.sum(X[:,:,:,None] * (w[None,None,:,:] + thetas[None,ids,:,:]), axis=2)
        if i % eval_delta == 0:
            print(i, 'eval test acc...', end='', flush=True)
            t = time.time()
            acc_test = ds.eval_test(w, thetas)
            print(acc_test, '({:.2f})'.format(time.time() - t))
            test_iters.append(i)
            accs_test.append(acc_test)

        thetas_grad = np.sum(X[:,:,:,None] * (pred - y)[:,:,None,:], axis=0) / ds.batch_size # [N,d,K]
        if steptheta > 0:
            thetas[ids] -= steptheta * thetas_grad

        thetas_grad /= np.clip(np.linalg.norm(thetas_grad, axis=(1,2)) / C, a_min=1., a_max=None)[:,None,None]
        w_grad = np.mean(thetas_grad, axis=0)
        if stepw > 0:
            w -= stepw * (w_grad + noise_mult * C * np.random.randn(ds.d, ds.n_classes) / L)
            priv.step(noise_multiplier=noise_mult, sample_rate=L/ds.N)

        eps, best_alpha = priv.get_privacy_spent(delta=delta)
        if i % eval_delta == 0:
            print('eps:', eps)
        epss.append(eps)
    return {'ls': ls, 'accs': accs, 'test_iters': test_iters, 'accs_test': accs_test, 'epss': epss}


# -----------------------------
# From main_nn.py (CNN feature extractor + PPSGD in feature space; JAX)
# -----------------------------
import numpy as np
import time
from opacus.accountants.rdp import RDPAccountant

from jax import grad
from jax import jit
from jax import random
from jax import vmap
from jax.example_libraries import stax
from jax.tree_util import tree_flatten, tree_unflatten
import jax.numpy as jnp

fdim = 32

init_random_params, predict_features = stax.serial(
    stax.Conv(16, (8, 8), padding='SAME', strides=(2, 2)),
    stax.Relu,
    stax.MaxPool((2, 2), (1, 1)),
    stax.Conv(32, (4, 4), padding='VALID', strides=(2, 2)),
    stax.Relu,
    stax.MaxPool((2, 2), (1, 1)),
    stax.Flatten,
    stax.Dense(fdim),
    stax.Relu,
)

def predict(params, w, theta, x):
    features = predict_features(params, x)
    return jnp.sum(features[:,:,None] * (w[None,:,:] + theta[None,:,:]), axis=1)

def loss(params, w, theta, x, y):
    return jnp.mean((predict(params, w, theta, x) - y) ** 2)

def clipped_grad(params, w, theta, l2_norm_clip, x, y):
    gp, gw, gth = grad(loss, (0, 1, 2))(params, w, theta, x, y)
    grads, tree_def = tree_flatten(gp)
    total_grad_norm = jnp.sqrt(jnp.sum(gw ** 2) + jnp.array([jnp.sum(g.ravel() ** 2) for g in grads]).sum())
    divisor = jnp.maximum(total_grad_norm / l2_norm_clip, 1.)
    normalized_grads = [g / divisor for g in grads]
    return tree_unflatten(tree_def, normalized_grads), gw / divisor, gth

@jit
def private_grad(params, w, thetas, xs, ys, rng, l2_norm_clip, noise_mult, num_users):
    cgp, cgw, gth = vmap(clipped_grad, (None, None, 0, None, 1, 1))(
        params, w, thetas, l2_norm_clip, xs, ys)
    # params gradient
    cgp_flat, grads_treedef = tree_flatten(cgp)
    cgp_tot = [g.mean(0) for g in cgp_flat]
    rngp, rngw = random.split(rng)
    rngs = random.split(rngp, len(cgp_tot))
    cgp_noised = [g + l2_norm_clip * noise_mult * random.normal(r, g.shape) / num_users
                  for r, g in zip(rngs, cgp_tot)]
    # w gradient
    cgw_tot = cgw.mean(0)
    cgw_noised = cgw_tot + l2_norm_clip * noise_mult * random.normal(rngw, cgw_tot.shape) / num_users
    return tree_unflatten(grads_treedef, cgp_noised), cgw_noised, gth


def training_priv(ds, step=0.5, stepw=None, steptheta=None, stepw_prob=None, 
                  C=10., noise_mult=0.05, delta=1e-4, eval_delta=500):
    key = random.PRNGKey(42)
    _, params = init_random_params(key, (-1, 28, 28, 1))
    w = np.random.randn(fdim, ds.n_classes) / np.sqrt(fdim)
    thetas = np.zeros((ds.N, fdim, ds.n_classes))

    priv = RDPAccountant()

    if stepw is None:
        stepw = step
    if steptheta is None:
        steptheta = step

    ls = []
    accs = []
    accs_test = []
    test_iters = []
    epss = []

    @jit
    def update_params(params, params_grad):
        ps, tree_def = tree_flatten(params)
        gs, gtree_def = tree_flatten(params_grad)
        ps_new = [p - stepw * g for (p, g) in zip(ps, gs)]
        return tree_unflatten(tree_def, ps_new)

    def eval_test(params, w, thetas):
        acc = 0
        cnt = 0
        for i, (X, y) in enumerate(ds.test_datasets):
            preds = predict(params, w, thetas[i], X.reshape(-1, 28, 28, 1))
            acc += np.sum(preds.argmax(1) == y)
            cnt += len(y)
        return acc / cnt

    for i, (ids, X, y) in enumerate(ds.batches()):
        L = len(ids)
        X = X[:,:L,:]  # [B,N,d]
        X = X.reshape(X.shape[0], X.shape[1], 28, 28, 1)
        y = y[:,:L,:]  # [B,N,K]
        y -= 0.1
        if i % eval_delta == 0:
            print(i, 'eval test acc...', end='', flush=True)
            t = time.time()
            acc_test = eval_test(params, w, thetas)
            print(acc_test, '({:.2f})'.format(time.time() - t))
            test_iters.append(i)
            accs_test.append(acc_test)

        rng = random.fold_in(key, i)  # new key per iteration
        params_grad, w_grad, thetas_grad = private_grad(params, w, thetas[ids],
                X, y, rng, C, noise_mult, L)
        if steptheta > 0:
            thetas[ids] -= L * steptheta * thetas_grad

        if stepw > 0:
            w -= stepw * w_grad
            params = update_params(params, params_grad)
            priv.step(noise_multiplier=noise_mult, sample_rate=L/ds.N)

        eps, best_alpha = priv.get_privacy_spent(delta=delta)
        if i % eval_delta == 0:
            print('eps:', eps)
        epss.append(eps)
    return {'ls': ls, 'accs': accs, 'test_iters': test_iters, 'accs_test': accs_test, 'epss': epss}
llm_extracted_info.experimental_info:
This repository implements the PPSGD method described in the Method section: additive per-user personalization with differentially private global aggregation. Below are the key experimental settings and how the algorithm is realized in code.

Core objective and model
- Additive personalization: per-user prediction uses w + Оё_i.
- Loss: squared loss on logits/scores.
  - Linear variants (synthetic and main_real): prediction = X В· (w + Оё_i).
  - CNN feature variant (main_nn): learn a shared CNN feature extractor ПҶ(x) with dimension fdim=32 and a linear layer; prediction uses ПҶ(x) В· (w + Оё_i).

Update rules (PPSGD)
- Per iteration, select a subset of users of size L (clients_per_round) from N users.
- For each selected user i, compute gradients on its local minibatch:
  - Compute gradient wrt Оё_i (local parameters) and take a local update: Оё_i вҶҗ Оё_i вҲ’ О·_Оё g_{Оё_i}.
  - Compute gradient wrt global parameters (w; and CNN params when present): g_{w,i}.
- Per-user clipping with norm C:
  - Linear variants: clip the per-user gradient equal to g_{w,i} by clipping the computed Оё_i gradient (they are identical for this model) using L2 norm and threshold C.
  - CNN variant (main_nn): compute per-user gradients for both CNN params and w, concatenate their norms with g_w and clip by C.
- Server aggregation and DP noise:
  - Average the clipped per-user global gradients.
  - Add Gaussian noise О¶ ~ N(0, (CВ·Пғ / L)^2 I) to the averaged gradient, where Пғ = noise_mult.
  - Update global parameters with step size О·_w.
- Personalization control via Оұ implemented through step sizes (О· base step):
  - 0 вүӨ Оұ вүӨ 1: stepw = Оұ В· step, steptheta = step (more personalization as Оұ вҶ’ 0).
  - Оұ = вҲ’1: global-only, stepw = step, steptheta = 0.
  - Оұ вүҘ 1: stepw = step, steptheta = step / Оұ (less personalization as Оұ increases).

Privacy accounting
- Uses Opacus RDPAccountant to compute (Оө, Оҙ) after each iteration.
- Client sampling amplification modeled by sample_rate = L / N when updating accountant.
- Оҙ is set via CLI (default 1e-4) and passed to get_privacy_spent.

Client sampling and minibatching
- clients_per_round = L (CLI configurable) users per iteration, sampled at random without replacement via dataset-specific iterators.
- Each user contributes a minibatch of size batch_size (CLI configurable, default 10).
- For main_real (EMNIST and Stackoverflow), batching is produced by ds.batches() that emits tuples (ids, X, y) with shapes [B, L, ...], where B = batch_size.

Hyperparameters and grids evaluated
- main_real.py grids
  - EMNIST grid:
    - step: [0.0001, 0.0002, 0.0005, 0.001, 0.002, 0.005, 0.01]
    - alpha: [0, 0.1, 0.3, 1., 3., 10., 30., 100., -1]
    - noise_mult: [2., 5., 20., 50., 70.]
    - C: [1., 10., 100.]
  - Stackoverflow grid:
    - step: [2., 5., 10., 20., 50., 100., 150., 200., 250.]
    - alpha: [0, 0.1, 0.3, 1., 3., 10., 30., 100., -1]
    - noise_mult: [0.02, 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, 2., 5.]
    - C: [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1., 10.]
- main_nn.py grids (CNN feature PPSGD; mainly tested with EMNIST-like 28Г—28 inputs)
  - EMNIST grid:
    - step: [0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1., 2., 5.]
    - alpha: [0, 0.1, 0.3, 1., 3., 10., 30., 100., -1]
    - noise_mult: [0, 0.1, 0.3, 1., 3., 10., 30., 100.]
    - C: [0.1, 1., 10.]
  - Stackoverflow grid:
    - step: [2., 5., 10., 20., 50., 100., 150., 200., 250.]
    - alpha: [0, 0.1, 0.3, 1., 3., 10., 30., 100., -1]
    - noise_mult: [0.02, 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, 2., 5.]
    - C: [0.01]
- main_synthetic.py grid (synthetic linear model)
  - step: [0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.7, 1., 1.2, 1.5, 1.8]
  - alpha: [0, 0.1, 0.3, 1., 3., 10., 30., 100., -1]
  - noise_mult: [0, 0.1, 0.3, 1., 3., 10., 30., 100., 300., 1000.]
  - C: [0.1, 1., 10., 100.]

Key CLI/default settings (where present)
- dataset selection: emnist or stackoverflow (main_real.py, main_nn.py).
- N (num users): default 3383 for EMNIST; 500 for Stackoverflow data pickles.
- clients_per_round (L): default 10 (main_real.py CLI default; EMNIST class itself defaults to 50 if constructed directly).
- batch size (B): default 10.
- num_epochs: default 1 (per-user epoch pass when preparing iterators for real datasets).
- DP parameters: delta default 1e-4, noise_mult from grid/CLI, clipping norm C from grid/CLI.

Architecture details for CNN feature variant (main_nn.py)
- Feature dimension fdim = 32.
- Architecture (stax): Conv(16, 8Г—8, stride 2) вҶ’ ReLU вҶ’ MaxPool(2Г—2) вҶ’ Conv(32, 4Г—4, stride 2) вҶ’ ReLU вҶ’ MaxPool вҶ’ Flatten вҶ’ Dense(32) вҶ’ ReLU.
- On top of features, per-class weights w вҲҲ R^{fdim Г— K} and per-user Оё_i of same shape; predictions sum features times (w + Оё_i).

DP calibration and reporting
- Gaussian mechanism implemented via additive noise to averaged gradients: noise standard deviation per coordinate equals (C В· noise_mult) / L for main_real and main_nn, and (C В· noise_mult) / N for the synthetic full-participation case.
- RDPAccountant called each iteration with sample_rate = L / N for real datasets and sample_rate = 1 for synthetic.
- Epsilon computed via get_privacy_spent(delta) and tracked over time; printed every eval_delta iterations in real/CNN runs.

Evaluation
- main_real.py: ds.eval_test(w, thetas) computes accuracy on test sets using global+personalized parameters.
- main_nn.py: eval_test runs the CNN-based predictor and computes accuracy.
- Tracking: test accuracy recorded at intervals eval_delta; epsilon values recorded each iteration.

Notes mapping to the Method description
- The code clips the per-user global gradient (g_{w,i}) by norm C and adds Gaussian noise in aggregation, matching the PPSGD prescription.
- Local Оё_i updates are private to users and not sent; DP is applied only to global parameter updates. This implements DP for global updates and supports JDP via billboard lemma when full personalization is used.
- Client sampling is supported via clients_per_round L and accounted for in the RDP accountant with sample_rate = L / N.
- Оұ tunes personalization through the ratio of step sizes for global vs local updates as specified above.

research_study_list.title: Renyi Differential Privacy of The Subsampled Shuffle Model In Distributed Learning
research_study_list.abstract:
We study privacy in a distributed learning framework, where clients
collaboratively build a learning model iteratively through interactions with a
server from whom we need privacy. Motivated by stochastic optimization and the
federated learning (FL) paradigm, we focus on the case where a small fraction
of data samples are randomly sub-sampled in each round to participate in the
learning process, which also enables privacy amplification. To obtain even
stronger local privacy guarantees, we study this in the shuffle privacy model,
where each client randomizes its response using a local differentially private
(LDP) mechanism and the server only receives a random permutation (shuffle) of
the clients' responses without their association to each client. The principal
result of this paper is a privacy-optimization performance trade-off for
discrete randomization mechanisms in this sub-sampled shuffle privacy model.
This is enabled through a new theoretical technique to analyze the Renyi
Differential Privacy (RDP) of the sub-sampled shuffle model. We numerically
demonstrate that, for important regimes, with composition our bound yields
significant improvement in privacy guarantee over the state-of-the-art
approximate Differential Privacy (DP) guarantee (with strong composition) for
sub-sampled shuffled models. We also demonstrate numerically significant
improvement in privacy-learning performance operating point using real data
sets.
research_study_list.full_text: Renyi Diп¬Җerential Privacy of the Subsampled Shuп¬„e Model in Distributed Learning Antonious M. Girgis, Deepesh Data, Suhas Diggavi Abstract We study privacy in a distributed learning framework, where clients collaboratively build a learning model iteratively through interactions with a server from whom we need privacy. Motivated by stochastic optimization and the federated learning (FL) paradigm, we focus on the case where a small fraction of data samples are randomly sub-sampled in each round to participate in the learning process, which also enables privacy ampliп¬Ғcation. To obtain even stronger local privacy guarantees, we study this in the shuп¬„e privacy model, where each client randomizes its response using a local diп¬Җerentially private (LDP) mechanism and the server only receives a random permutation (shuп¬„e) of the clientsвҖҷ responses without their association to each client. The principal result of this paper is a privacy-optimization performance trade-oп¬Җ for discrete randomization mechanisms in this sub-sampled shuп¬„e privacy model. This is enabled through a new theoretical technique to analyze the Renyi Diп¬Җerential Privacy (RDP) of the sub-sampled shuп¬„e model. We numerically demonstrate that, for important regimes, with composition our bound yields signiп¬Ғcant improvement in privacy guarantee over the state-of-the-art approximate Diп¬Җerential Privacy (DP) guarantee (with strong composition) for sub-sampled shuп¬„ed models. We also demonstrate numerically signiп¬Ғcant improvement in privacy-learning performance operating point using real data sets. 1 Introduction As learning moves towards the edge, there is a need to collaborate to build learning models 1, such as in federated learning [KMY+16, YLCT19, KMA+19]. In this framework, the collaboration is typically mediated by a server. In particular, we want to collaboratively build a learning model by solving an empirical risk minimization (ERM) problem (see (4) in Section 2). To obtain a model parametrized by Оё using ERM, the commonly used mechanism is Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) [Bot10]. However, one needs to solve this while enabling strong privacy guarantees on local data from the server, while also obtaining good learning performance, i.e., a suitable privacy-learning performance operating point. Diп¬Җerential privacy (DP) [DMNS06] is the gold standard notion of data privacy that gives a rigorous framework through quantifying the information leakage about individual training data points from the observed interactions. Though DP was originally proposed in a framework where data resides centrally [DMNS06], for distributed learning the more appropriate notion is of local diп¬Җerential privacy (LDP) [KLN +11, DJW13]. Here, each client randomizes its interactions with the server from whom the data is to be kept private (e.g., see industrial implementations [EPK14, Gre16, DKY17]). However, LDP mechanisms suп¬Җer from poor performance in comparison with the central DP mechanisms [DJW13,KLN +11,KBR16]. To overcome this, a new privacy framework using anonymization has been proposed in the so-called shuп¬„ed model [EFM+19, GGK+19, BBGN19b, GPV19, BBGN19a, CSU+19, BBGN19c, BBGN20]. In the shuп¬„ed model, each client sends her private message to a secure shuп¬„er that randomly permutes all the received messages before forwarding them to the server. This model enables signiп¬Ғcantly better privacy-utility performance by amplifying DP through this shuп¬„ing. Therefore, in this paper we consider the shuп¬„e privacy framework for distributed learning. 1This is because no client has access to enough data to build rich learning models locally and we do not want to directly share local data. 1 arXiv:2107.08763v1  [cs.LG]  19 Jul 2021Shuffler рқ‘‘1 рқ‘‘2 рқ‘‘3 рқ‘‘4 рқ‘‘5 Figure 1: An iteration from the CLDP-SGD Algorithm, where 3 clients are randomly chosen at each iteration. Each client sends the private gradient Rp(gt(di)) to the shuп¬„er that randomly permutes the gradients before passing them to the server. In solving (4) using (distributed) gradient descent, each exchange leaks information about the local data, but we need as many steps as possible to obtain a good model; setting up the tension between privacy and performance. The goal is to obtain as many such interactions as possible for a given privacy budget. This is quantiп¬Ғed through analyzing the privacy of the composition of privacy mechanisms. Abadi et al. [ACG+16] developed a framework for tighter analysis of such compositions, and this was later reformulated in terms of Renyi Diп¬Җerential Privacy (RDP) [Mir17], and mapping this back to DP guarantee [MTZ19]. Therefore, studying RDP is important to obtaining strong composition privacy results, and is the focus of this paper. In distributed (and federated) learning, a fraction of the data samples are sampled; for example, with random client participation and stochastic gradient descent (SGD), which can be written as Оёt+1 вҶҗОёt вҲ’О·t 1 |I| вҲ‘ iвҲҲI R(вҲҮfi(Оёt)), where Ris the local randomization mechanism and Iare the indices of the sampled data. This is a subsampled mechanism that enables another privacy ampliп¬Ғcation opportunity; which, in several cases, is shown to yield a privacy advantage proportional to the subsampling rate; see [KLN +11,Ull17]. The central technical question addressed in this paper is how to analyze the RDP of an arbitrary discrete mechanism for the subsampled shuп¬„e privacy model. This enables us to answer the overall question posed in this paper, which is an achievable privacy-learning performance trade-oп¬Җ point for solving (4) in the shuп¬„ed privacy model for distributed learning (see Figure 1). Our contributions are: вҖў We analyze the RDP of subsampled mechanisms in the shuп¬„e framework by developing a novel bound applicable to any discrete Пө0-LDP mechanism as a function of the RDP order О», subsampling rate Оі, the LDP parameter Пө0, and the number of clients n; see Theorem 1. The bound is explicit and amenable to numerics, including all constants.2 Furthermore, the bounds are valid for generic LDP mechanisms and all parameter regimes.3 We also provide a lower bound for the RDP in Theorem 2. We prove our upper bound (Theorem 1) using the following novel analysis techniques: First, we reduce the problem of computing the RDP of sub-sampled shuп¬„e mechanisms to the problem of computing ternary |ПҮ|Оұ-DP [WBK19] of shuп¬„e (non sub-sampled) mechanisms; see Lemma 2. Then we reduce the computation of the ternary |ПҮ|Оұ-DP of shuп¬„e mechanisms for a generic triple of neighboring datasets to those that have a special structure (see Theorem 5) вҖ“ this reduction step is one of the core technical results of this paper. Then we bound the ternary |ПҮ|Оұ-DP of the shuп¬„e mechanisms for triples of neighboring datasets having special structures by bounding the Pearson-Vajda divergence [WBK19] using some concentration properties (see Theorem 6). вҖў Using the core technical result in Theorem 1, we analyze privacy-convergence trade-oп¬Җs of the CLDP-SGD algorithm (see Algorithm 1) for Lipschitz convex functions in Theorem 3. This partially resolves an open 2As emphasized in [WBK19], вҖңin diп¬Җerential privacy, constants matterвҖқ. 3Some of the best known approximate DP bounds for the shuп¬„e model [BBGN19c,FMT20] are restricted to certain parameter regimes in terms of n,Оҙ,Пө0, etc. 2question posed in [GDD +21b], to extend their privacy analysis to RDP and signiп¬Ғcantly strengthening their privacy guarantees. вҖў Numerically, we save a factor of 14 Г—in privacy (Пө) over the best known results for approximate DP for shuп¬„ing [FMT20] combined with strong composition [KOV15] for T = 105,Оі = 0.001,n = 106. Translating these to privacy-performance operating point in distributed optimization, over the MNIST data set with в„“вҲһ- norm clipping we numerically show gains: For the same privacy budget of Пө= 1.4, we get a test performance of 80% whereas using strong composition the test performance of [FMT20] is 70%; furthermore, we achieve 90% accuracy with the total privacy budget Пө= 2.91, whereas, [FMT20] (with strong composition) achieve the same accuracy with a total privacy budget of Пө= 4.82. See Section 4 for more results. Related work: We give a more complete literature review in Appendix A, and focus here on the works that are closest to the results presented in this paper. вҖў Private optimization in the shuп¬„ed model: Recently, [EFM+20] and [GDD+21b] have proposed diп¬Җerentially private SGD algorithms for federated learning, where at each iteration, each client applies an LDP mechanism on the gradients with the existence of a secure shuп¬„er between the clients and the central server. However, the privacy analyses in these works developed approximate DP using advanced composition theorems for DP (e.g., [DRV10,KOV15]), which are known to be loose for composition [ACG +16]. To the best of our knowledge, analyzing the private optimization framework using RDP and subsampling in the shuп¬„ed model is new to this paper. вҖў Subsampled RDP: The works [MTZ19,WBK19,ZW19] have studied the RDP of subsampled mechanisms without shuп¬„ing. They demonstrated that this provides a tighter bound on the total privacy loss than the bound that can be obtained using the standard strong composition theorems. The RDP of the shuп¬„ed model was very recently studied in [GDD +21a], but without incorporating subsampling, which poses new technical challenges, as directly bounding the RDP of subsampled shuп¬„e mechanisms is non-trivial. We overcome this by reducing our problem of computing RDP to bounding the ternary |ПҮ|Оұ-DP, and bounding the latter is a core technical contribution of our paper. Paper organization: We give preliminaries and problem formulation in Section 2, main results (upper and lower bounds, and privacy-convergence tradeoп¬Җ) in Section 3, numerical results in Section 4, proof of the upper bound in Section 5, and proof of the ternary DP of the shuп¬„e model in Section 6. 2 Preliminaries and Problem Formulation We use several privacy deп¬Ғnitions throughout this paper. However, we numerically compute it for several interesting regimes of parameters in our numerical experiments; see Section 4 for more details. 4 Numerical Results In this section, we present numerical experiments to show the performance of our bounds on RDP of the subsampled shuп¬„e mechanism and its usage for getting approximate DP of Algorithm 1 for training machine learning models. PMLR, 2019. 20A Literature Review We give the most relevant work related to the paper and review some of the main developments in diп¬Җerentially private learning below. Private Optimization: In [CMS11], Chaudhuri et al. studied centralized privacy-preserving machine learning algorithms for convex optimization problem. In [BST14], Bassily et al. derived lower bounds on the empirical risk minimization under central diп¬Җerential privacy constraints. Furthermore, they proposed a diп¬Җerential privacy SGD algorithm that matches the lower bound for convex functions. In [ACG +16], the authors have generalized the private SGD algorithm proposed in [BST14] for non-convex optimization framework. In addition, the authors have proposed a new analysis technique, called moment accounting, to improve on the strong composition theorems to compute the central diп¬Җerential privacy guarantee for iterative algorithms. However, the works mentioned, [CMS11,BST14,ACG +16], assume that there exists a trusted server that collects the clientsвҖҷ data. This motivates other works to design a distributed SGD algorithms, where each client perturbs her own data without needing a trusted server. Distributed learning under local diп¬Җerential privacy (LDP) has studied in [ASY +18, EFM+20, GDD+21b]. In [ASY+18] the authors proposed a communication-eп¬ғcient algorithm for learning models under local diп¬Җerential privacy. In [EFM+20], the authors have proposed a distributed local-diп¬Җerential-privacy gradient descent algorithm, a newly proposed anonymization/shuп¬„ing framework [BBGN19c] is used to amplify the privacy. In [GDD+21b], the authors proposed communication eп¬ғcient algorithms for general в„“p-norm stetting under local diп¬Җerential privacy constraints, where they use recent results on ampliп¬Ғcation by shuп¬„ing to boost the privacy-utility trade-oп¬Җs of the distributed learning algorithms. Shuп¬„ed privacy model: The shuп¬„ed model of privacy has been of signiп¬Ғcant recent interest [EFM +19, GGK+19,BBGN19b, GPV19,BBGN19a, CSU+19,BBGN19c,BBGN20]. However, most of the existing works in literature [EFM +19, BBGN19c, FMT20] only characterize the approximate DP of the shuп¬„ed model. Recently, the authors in [GDD+21a] proposed a novel bound on the RDP of the shuп¬„ed model, where they show that the RDP provides a signiп¬Ғcant saving in computing the total privacy budget for a composition of a sequence of shuп¬„ed mechanisms. However, the work [GDD +21a] does not characterize the RDP of the subsampled shuп¬„e mechanism. We can compute a bound on the RDP of the subsampled shuп¬„e mechanism by combining the bound of the RDP of the shuп¬„e mechanism in [GDD +21a] with the bound of the subsampled RDP mechanism in [WBK19]. However, we show numerically that our new bound on the subsampled shuп¬„e mechanism outperforms this bound. Renyi diп¬Җerential privacy: The work of Abadi et al. [ACG+16] provided a new analysis technique to improve on the strong composition theorems. Inherently, this used Renyi divergence, and was later formalized in [Mir17] which deп¬Ғned Renyi diп¬Җerential privacy (RDP). Several works [MTZ19,WBK19,ZW19] have shown that analyzing the RDP of subsampled mechanisms provides a tighter bound on the total privacy loss than the bound that can be obtained using the standard strong composition theorems. In this paper, we analyze the RDP of the subsampled shuп¬„e model, where we can bound the approximate DP of a sequence of subsampled shuп¬„e models using the transformation from RDP to approximate DP [ACG +16,WBK19, CKS20,ALC+21]. We show that our RDP analysis provides a better bound on the total privacy loss of composition than the bound that can be obtained using the standard strong composition theorems and the bound that can be obtained by combining the RDP bound of the shuп¬„e model in [GDD +21a] with the subsampled RDP mechanism in [WBK19]. 21B Completing the Proof of Theorem 4 For simplicity of notation, for any mвҲҲ{0,1,...,n вҲ’1}, deп¬Ғne qm := (kвҲ’1 m ) qm(1 вҲ’q)kвҲ’mвҲ’1 Em := EhвҲјMsh(DвҖІвҖІ(k) m+1) [вҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗ Msh(D(k) m+1)(h) вҲ’Msh(DвҖІ(k) m+1)(h) Msh(DвҖІвҖІ(k) m+1)(h) вҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗвҸҗ Оұ] . llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: The paper introduces a new analysis to bound the Renyi Differential Privacy (RDP) of subsampled shuffle mechanisms for arbitrary discrete Оө0-LDP local randomizers. The key results are: (1) an explicit, constant-tight upper bound on the RDP of the subsampled shuffle model as a function of RDP order О», subsampling rate Оі, local privacy Оө0, and number of clients n (Theorem 1); (2) a complementary lower bound (Theorem 2); (3) application to a client-level LDP-SGD (CLDP-SGD) algorithm with a rigorous privacyвҖ“convergence trade-off (Theorem 3), yielding better composition guarantees than prior approximate DP analyses; and (4) numerical evidence showing significant privacy improvements (up to 14Г— smaller Оө for typical FL settings) and improved accuracyвҖ“privacy operating points on MNIST compared to prior state-of-the-art shuffle amplification plus strong composition analyses.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Privacy analysis: Reduce the problem of RDP for subsampled shuffle to bounding ternary |ПҮ|_Оұ-DP of the (non-subsampled) shuffle mechanism. Then reduce arbitrary neighboring dataset triples to specially structured triples (Theorem 5). Bound ternary |ПҮ|_Оұ-DP using PearsonвҖ“Vajda divergence and concentration arguments (Theorem 6), leading to an explicit RDP upper bound (Theorem 1) and a lower bound via a binary randomized response construction (Theorem 2).
- Composition and conversion: Use RDP adaptive composition to aggregate privacy across T rounds and convert RDP to (Оө,Оҙ)-DP via standard RDPвҶ’DP conversion (Lemma 1).
- Algorithmic framework (CLDP-SGD): At each round, uniformly subsample k clients (Оі=k/n); each client computes and в„“_p-clips a gradient, applies an unbiased discrete Оө0-LDP mechanism Rp with bounded variance, sends output to a secure shuffler; the server averages shuffled reports and updates parameters. Analyze convergence for L-Lipschitz convex losses w.r.t. the в„“_g dual norm using standard SGD analysis, yielding O(D G log T / вҲҡT) with G^2 = max{d^{1вҲ’2/p},1}L^2 + G_p(L)/(Оіn).
- Practical accounting: Optimize RDP order О» numerically to obtain tight (Оө,Оҙ) for the composed mechanism.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Privacy-accounting comparisons: Compute total (Оө,Оҙ)-DP for T-fold compositions under Оҙ=1e-8 across settings varying T, n, Оі, Оө0. Plots compare: (i) proposed RDP upper and lower bounds; (ii) baseline of shuffle amplification from [FMT20] combined with subsampling [Ull17] and strong composition [KOV15]. Example regimes include Оө0вҲҲ{1,2,3}, ОівҲҲ{0.001,0.0001}, T up to 1e6, n up to 1e8. Results show up to 14Г— smaller Оө for T=1e5, Оі=0.001, n=1e6.
- Learning experiments (MNIST): 60k train/10k test; CNN architecture: two conv layers (16 filters 8Г—8 stride 2; max-pool 2Г—2; 16 filters 4Г—4 stride 2; max-pool 2Г—2), followed by FC-32 and softmax-10 (~13,170 params).
  вҖў в„“вҲһ-clipping: n=60,000 clients (one sample each), sample 10,000 per round, clip C=1/100, Rp=R_вҲһ with Оө0=1.5; 200 epochs; Оҙ=1e-5; LR 0.3 for 70 epochs then 0.18; averaged over 10 runs. AccuracyвҖ“privacy: 80% at Оө=1.4 (vs 70.7% baseline); 90% at Оө=2.91 (vs 4.82 baseline).
  вҖў в„“2-clipping: sample 2,000 per round, clip C=0.005, Rp=PrivUnit (R2) with Оө0=2; 200 epochs; Оҙ=1e-5; LR 12 (30 epochs) вҶ’ 4 (30) вҶ’ 3.5 (rest); averaged over 4 runs. AccuracyвҖ“privacy: 81.15% at Оө=3 (vs 76.46% baseline); 89.7% at Оө=5.8 (vs 18.3 baseline).
- Privacy budgets obtained by optimizing RDP order О» using Theorem 1; baselines follow [FMT20] shuffle amplification + subsampling + strong composition.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Analysis targets discrete-output Оө0-LDP mechanisms; continuous mechanisms are not directly covered by the main theorems.
- Lower bound (Theorem 2) is derived for a specific binary randomized response setting; tightness of the gap vs the upper bound for general mechanisms is not fully characterized.
- Convergence guarantees assume convex, L-Lipschitz losses and unbiased local randomizers; they do not cover non-convex deep networks theoretically (experiments are empirical only).
- Requires a trusted secure shuffler and exact knowledge of participation/subsampling; robustness to shuffler failures or partial anonymity is not analyzed.
- Final (Оө,Оҙ) requires numerical optimization over RDP order О»; no closed-form optimal О».
- Empirical validation is limited to MNIST and relatively small models; broader tasks, datasets, and heterogeneous FL settings are not evaluated.
- Communication/compute overheads and systems aspects (e.g., latency, dropouts, stragglers) are not analyzed.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Extend bounds to continuous-output LDP mechanisms and more general local randomizers (e.g., Gaussian mechanisms) in the subsampled shuffle model.
- Tighten gaps between upper and lower bounds; develop mechanism-specific lower bounds and explore optimality of constants.
- Analyze non-convex optimization (deep learning) theoretically under the subsampled shuffle model and provide stability-based generalization guarantees.
- Study robustness to imperfect or adversarial shufflers, multi-shuffler pipelines, and integration with secure aggregation.
- Explore alternative subsampling schemes (Poisson subsampling), adaptive client sampling, and heterogeneous participation.
- Provide practical accounting libraries implementing the new RDP bounds and their composition for real-world FL systems.
- Empirically validate on larger-scale datasets and tasks (e.g., CIFAR/ImageNet, NLP), and under realistic FL heterogeneity and communication constraints.
- Investigate communication-efficient mechanisms jointly optimized for privacyвҖ“utilityвҖ“bandwidth under the proposed RDP framework.

research_study_list.title: SoteriaFL: A Unified Framework for Private Federated Learning with Communication Compression
research_study_list.abstract:
To enable large-scale machine learning in bandwidth-hungry environments such
as wireless networks, significant progress has been made recently in designing
communication-efficient federated learning algorithms with the aid of
communication compression. On the other end, privacy-preserving, especially at
the client level, is another important desideratum that has not been addressed
simultaneously in the presence of advanced communication compression techniques
yet. In this paper, we propose a unified framework that enhances the
communication efficiency of private federated learning with communication
compression. Exploiting both general compression operators and local
differential privacy, we first examine a simple algorithm that applies
compression directly to differentially-private stochastic gradient descent, and
identify its limitations. We then propose a unified framework SoteriaFL for
private federated learning, which accommodates a general family of local
gradient estimators including popular stochastic variance-reduced gradient
methods and the state-of-the-art shifted compression scheme. We provide a
comprehensive characterization of its performance trade-offs in terms of
privacy, utility, and communication complexity, where SoteraFL is shown to
achieve better communication complexity without sacrificing privacy nor utility
than other private federated learning algorithms without communication
compression.
research_study_list.full_text: SoteriaFL: A Uniп¬Ғed Framework for Private Federated Learning with Communication Compression Zhize LiвҲ— CMU Haoyu ZhaoвҖ  Princeton Boyue LiвҲ— CMU Yuejie ChiвҲ— CMU June 2022; Revised October 2022 Abstract To enable large-scale machine learning in bandwidth-hungry environments such as wireless networks, signiп¬Ғcant progress has been made recently in designing communication-eп¬ғcient federated learning algorithms with the aid of communication compression. On the other end, privacy-preserving, especially at the client level, is another important desideratum that has not been addressed simultaneously in the presence of advanced communication compression techniques yet. In this paper, we propose a uniп¬Ғed framework that enhances the communication eп¬ғciency of private federated learning with communication compression. Exploiting both general compression operators and local diп¬Җerential privacy, we п¬Ғrst examine a simple algorithm that applies compression directly to diп¬Җerentially-private stochastic gradient descent, and identify its limitations. We then propose a uniп¬Ғed frameworkSoteriaFL for private federated learning, which accommodates a general family of local gradient estimators including popular stochastic variance-reduced gradient methods and the state-of-the-art shifted compression scheme. We provide a comprehensive characterization of its performance trade-oп¬Җs in terms of privacy, utility, and communication complexity, whereSoteriaFL is shown to achieve better communication complexity without sacriп¬Ғcing privacy nor utility than other private federated learning algorithms without communication compression. Keywords: federated learning, local diп¬Җerential privacy, communication compression, uniп¬Ғed framework 1 Introduction With the proliferation of mobile and edge devices, federated learning (FL) [42, 55] has recently emerged as a disruptive paradigm for training large-scale machine learning models over a vast amount of geographically distributed and heterogeneous devices. For instance, Google uses FL in the Gboard mobile keyboard for next word predictions [29]. FL is often modeled as a distributed optimization problem [41, 42, 55, 35, 72], aiming to solve min xвҲҲRd { f(x; D) := 1 n nвҲ‘ i=1 f(x; Di) } , where f(x; Di) := 1 m mвҲ‘ j=1 f(x; di,j). (1) Here, D denotes the entire dataset distributed across alln clients, where each clienti has a local dataset Di = {di,j}m j=1 of equal sizem,1 xвҲҲRd denotes the model parameters,f(x; D), f(x; Di), and f(x; di,j) denote the nonconvex loss function of the current modelxon the entire datasetD, the local datasetDi, and a single data sampledi,j, respectively. For simplicity, we usef(x), fi(x) and fi,j(x) to denotef(x; D), f(x; Di) and f(x; di,j), respectively. вҲ—Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Carnegie Mellon University, Pittsburgh, PA 15213, USA; Emails: {zhizel, boyuel, yuejiec}@andrew.cmu.edu. вҖ Department of Computer Science, Princeton University, Princeton, NJ 08540, USA; Email:haoyu@princeton.edu. 1This is without loss of generality, since otherwise one can simply adjust the weights of the loss function. 1 arXiv:2206.09888v2  [cs.LG]  12 Oct 20221.1 Motivation: privacy-utility-communication trade-oп¬Җs To unleash the full potential of FL, it is extremely important that the algorithm designed to solve (1) needs to meet several competing desiderata. Communication eп¬ғciency. Communication between the server and clients is well recognized as the main bottleneck for optimizing the latency of FL systems, especially when the clientsвҖ”such as mobile devicesвҖ”have limited bandwidth, the number of clients is large, and/or the machine learning model has a lot of parametersвҖ”for example, the language model GPT-3 [7] has billions of parameters and therefore cumbersome to share directly. Therefore, it is very important to design FL algorithms to reduce the overall communication cost, which takes into account boththe number of communication roundsand the cost per communication roundfor reaching a desired accuracy. With these two quantities in mind, there are two principal approaches for communication-eп¬ғcient FL: 1)local methods, where in each communication round, clients run multiple local update steps before communicating with the server, in the hope of reducing the number of communication rounds, e.g., [55, 48, 39, 27, 38, 71, 60, 9, 47, 46, 2, 78, 58, 57]; 2)compression methods, where clients send compressed communication message to the server, in the hope of reducing the cost per communication round, e.g., [4, 40, 70, 31, 37, 56, 61, 52, 28, 51, 62, 21, 45, 77, 79, 63]. While both categories have garnered signiп¬Ғcant attention in recent years, we focus on the second approach based on communication compression to enhance communication eп¬ғciency. Privacy preserving. While FL holds great promise of harnessing the inferential power of private data stored on a large number of distributed clients, these local data at clients often contain sensitive or proprietary information without consent to share. Although FL may appear to protect the data privacy via storing data locally and only sharing the model updates (e.g., gradient information), the training process can nonetheless reveal sensitive information as demonstrated by, e.g., Zhu et al.[81]. It is thus desirable for FL to preserve privacy in a guaranteed manner [24, 35, 64, 72]. To ensure the training process does not accidentally leak private information, advanced privacy-preserving tools such asdiп¬Җerential privacy(DP) [20] have been widely integrated into training algorithms [18, 12, 19, 1, 69, 32, 15, 23]. A notable example is Abadi et al.[1], which developed a diп¬Җerentially-private stochastic gradient descent (SGD) algorithmDP-SGD in the centralized (single-node) setting. More recently, several diп¬Җerentially-private algorithms [33, 73, 65, 54] are proposed for the more general distributed (n-node) setting suitable for FL. In this paper, we also follow the DP approach to preserve privacy. In particular, we adopt local diп¬Җerential privacy (LDP) to respect the privacy of each client, which is critical in FL. Goal. Encouraged by recent advances in communication compression techniques, and the widespread success of diп¬Җerentially-private methods, a natural question is Can we develop a uniп¬Ғed framework for private federated learning with communication compression, and understand the trade-oп¬Җs between privacy, utility, and communication? Note that there have been a handful of works that simultaneously address compression and privacy in FL. Unfortunately, they only provide partial answers to the above question. Most of the existing works only consider speciп¬Ғc, elementary, or tailored compression schemes that are applied directly to the gradient messages in DP-SGD [3, 74, 26, 82, 76, 17]. A number of works [66, 13, 14, 36, 22, 67] extended and considered diп¬Җerent compression schemes, but did not provide concrete trade-oп¬Җs in terms of privacy, utility and communication. Furthermore, existing theoretical analyses can be limited only to convex problems [26], lacking in some aspects such as utility [82], or delivering pessimistic guarantees on utility and/or communication due to strong assumptions [76, 17]. Finally, existing work only studied the DP framework for direct compression, while it is known that the recently developed shifted compression scheme [56, 30, 50] achieves much better convergence guarantees. Due to noise injection for privacy-preserving, it is a priori unclear if the shifted compression scheme is also compatible with privacy. 2Table 1: Comparisons among (local) diп¬Җerentially-private algorithms for the nonconvex problem (1) in both central (single-node) and distributed (n-node) settings. Here,mdenotes the number of data stored on a single client, n is the number of clients,d is the dimension, andПү is the parameter for the compression operator (cf. Deп¬Ғnition 1). The communication complexity is computed byndT/(1 +Пү), whereT is the total number of communication rounds, andnd/(1 +Пү) is the communication cost per round. The utility / accuracy measures the average squared gradient norm of the objective function afterT rounds. Note that the algorithm is better when the utility/accuracy and the communication complexity are small under the same privacy guarantee. Algorithm Privacy Utility/Accuracy Communication Complexity Remark RPPSGD [75] (Пө,Оҙ)-DP вҲҡ dlog(m/Оҙ) log(1/Оҙ) mПө вҖ” single node DP-GD/SGD [1, 69] (Пө,Оҙ)-DP вҲҡ dlog(1/Оҙ) mПө вҖ” single node DP-SRM [73] (Пө,Оҙ)-DP вҲҡ dlog(1/Оҙ) mПө вҖ” single node Distributed DP-SRM [73] (1) (Пө,Оҙ)-DP вҲҡ dlog(1/Оҙ) nmПө n2mПө вҲҡ dвҲҡ log(1/Оҙ) n nodes, no compression LDP SVRG LDP SPIDER [54] (Пө,Оҙ)-LDP вҲҡ dlog(1/Оҙ)вҲҡnmПө n3/2mПө вҲҡ dвҲҡ log(1/Оҙ) n nodes, no compression Q-DPSGD-1 [17] (2) (Пө,Оҙ)-LDP (ЛңПғ2/n+1/m)2/3(dlog(1/Оҙ))1/3 m2/3Пө2/3 (1+n/(mЛңПғ2))m2Пө2 dlog(1/Оҙ) n nodes, direct compression SDM-DSGD [76] (3) (Пө,Оҙ)-LDP ЛңO (вҲҡ dlog(1/Оҙ)вҲҡnmПө ) n7/2mПө вҲҡ d (1+Пү)3/2вҲҡ log(1/Оҙ) + nm2Пө2 (1+Пү) log(1/Оҙ) n nodes, direct compression CDP-SGD (Theorem 1) (Пө,Оҙ)-LDP вҲҡ (1+Пү)dlog(1/Оҙ)вҲҡnmПө n3/2mПө вҲҡ d (1+Пү)3/2вҲҡ log(1/Оҙ) + nm2Пө2 (1+Пү) log(1/Оҙ) n nodes, direct compression SoteriaFL-SGD SoteriaFL-GD (Corollary 1) (4) (Пө,Оҙ)-LDP вҲҡ (1+Пү)dlog(1/Оҙ)вҲҡnmПө (1 +вҲҡП„) n3/2mПө вҲҡ d (1+Пү)3/2вҲҡ log(1/Оҙ) (1 +вҲҡП„) n nodes, shifted compression SoteriaFL-SVRG SoteriaFL-SAGA (Corollary 2, 3) (4) (Пө,Оҙ)-LDP вҲҡ (1+Пү)dlog(1/Оҙ)вҲҡnmПө n3/2mПө вҲҡ d (1+Пү)3/2вҲҡ log(1/Оҙ) (1 +П„) n nodes, shifted compression (1) Wang et al.[73] considered the вҖңglobalвҖқ(Пө,Оҙ)-DP (which only protects the privacy for entire datasetD, i.e., the local datasetDi on nodei may leak to other nodesj Мё= i) without communication compression. However, we consider the вҖңlocalвҖқ(Пө,Оҙ)-LDP which can protect the local datasetsDiвҖҷs at the client level. (2) Ding et al.[17] adopted a slightly diп¬Җerent compression assumptionE[вҲҘC(x) вҲ’xвҲҘ2] вүӨЛңПғ2, with ЛңПғ2 play- ing a similar role as(1 +Пү) in ours. However, it obtains a worse accuracy (ЛңПғ2/n+1/m)2/3(dlog(1/Оҙ))1/3 m2/3Пө2/3 =вҲҡ (ЛңПғ2/n+1/m)dlog(1/Оҙ) mПө В· ( (ЛңПғ2/n+1/m)m2Пө2 dlog(1/Оҙ) )1/6 = вҲҡ (ЛңПғ2/n+1/m)dlog(1/Оҙ) mПө В·T1/6, a factor of T1/6 worse than the utility of the other algorithms including ours, whereT = (ЛңПғ2/n+1/m)m2Пө2 dlog(1/Оҙ) is the optimal choice to achieve the best accuracy forQ-DPSGD-1. (3) Zhang et al.[76] only considered random-k sparsiп¬Ғcation, which is a special case of our general compression operator. Moreover, it requires 1 +Пү вүӘlog T, i.e., at least k вү« d log T out of d coordinates need to be communicated, and its utility hides logarithmic factors larger than1 +Пү. The communication complexityn7/2 is due to their convergence conditionT >n5. (4) Here, П„ := (1+Пү)3/2 n1/2 . IfnвүҘ(1 +Пү)3 (which is typical in FL), thenП„ <1, and we can drop the terms involving П„ from SoteriaFL. 1.2 Our contributions In this paper, we answer the above question by providing a general approach that enhances the communication eп¬ғciency of private federated learning in thenonconvex setting, through a uniп¬Ғed framework calledSoteriaFL (see Algorithm 2). Speciп¬Ғcally, we have the following contributions. 1. We п¬Ғrst present a simple algorithmCDP-SGD (Algorithm 1) that directly combines communication compression andDP-SGD. We provide theoretical analysis forCDP-SGD in Theorem 1 and show its limitations in communication eп¬ғciency. 2. We then propose a general frameworkSoteriaFL for private FL, which accommodates a general family of local gradient estimators including popular stochastic variance-reduced gradient methods and the 3state-of-the-art shifted compression scheme. We provide a uniп¬Ғed characterization of its performance trade-oп¬Җs in terms of privacy, utility (convergence accuracy), and communication complexity. 3. We apply our uniп¬Ғed analysis forSoteriaFL and obtain theoretical guarantees for several new private FL algorithms, includingSoteriaFL-GD, SoteriaFL-SGD, SoteriaFL-SVRG, andSoteriaFL-SAGA. All of these algorithms are shown to perform better than the plainCDP-SGD (Algorithm 1), and have lower communication complexity compared with other private FL algorithms without compression. The numerical experiments also corroborate the theory and conп¬Ғrm the practical superiority ofSoteriaFL. We provide detailed comparisons between the proposed approach and prior arts in Table 1. To the best of our knowledge,SoteriaFL is the п¬Ғrst uniп¬Ғed framework that simultaneously enables local diп¬Җerential privacy and shifted compression, and allows п¬Ӯexible local computation protocols at the client level. 2 Preliminaries Let [n] denote the set{1,2,В·В·В· ,n}and вҲҘВ·вҲҘdenote the Euclidean norm of a vector. LetвҹЁu,vвҹ©denote the standard Euclidean inner product of two vectorsuand v. Let fвҲ—:= minx f(x) >вҲ’вҲһdenote the optimal value of the objective function in (1). In addition, we use the standard order notationO(В·) to hide absolute constants. We now introduce the deп¬Ғnitions of the compression operator and local diп¬Җerential privacy, as well as some standard assumptions for the objective functions. Compression operator. Let us introduce the notion of a randomizedcompression operator, which is used to compress the gradients to save communication. The following deп¬Ғnition of unbiased compressors is standard and has been used in many distributed/federated learning algorithms [4, 40, 56, 30, 50, 52, 28, 51]. Deп¬Ғnition 1(Compression operator). A randomized mapC: Rd вҶҰвҶ’Rd is anПү-compression operator if for all xвҲҲRd, it satisп¬Ғes E[C(x)] = x, E [ вҲҘC(x) вҲ’xвҲҘ2 ] вүӨПүвҲҘxвҲҘ2 . (2) In particular, no compression (C(x) вүЎx) impliesПү= 0. Note that the conditions (2) are satisп¬Ғed by many practically useful compression operators, e.g., random sparsiп¬Ғcation and random quantization [4, 52, 51]. A useful rule of thumb is that the communication cost is often reduced by a factor of1 1+Пү due to compression [4]. Next, we brieп¬Ӯy discuss an example called random sparsiп¬Ғcation to provide more intuition. Example 1(Random sparsiп¬Ғcation). Given xвҲҲRd, the random-k sparsiп¬Ғcation operator is deп¬Ғned by C(x) := d k В·(Оҫk вҠҷx), whereвҠҷdenotes the Hadamard (element-wise) product andОҫk вҲҲ{0,1}d is a uniformly random binary vector withk nonzero entries (вҲҘОҫkвҲҘ0 = k). This random-k sparsiп¬Ғcation operatorCsatisп¬Ғes (2) withПү= d k вҲ’1, and the communication cost is reduced by a factor of1 1+Пү since we transmitk= d 1+Пү (due toПү= d k вҲ’1) coordinates rather thand coordinates of the message. Local diп¬Җerential privacy. We not only want to train the machine learning model using fewer communi- cation bits, but also want to maintain each clientвҖҷs local privacy, which is a key component for FL applications. Following the framework of (local) diп¬Җerential privacy [5, 11, 80], we say that two datasetsD and DвҖІ are neighbors if they diп¬Җer by only one entry. We have the following deп¬Ғnition for local diп¬Җerential privacy (LDP). Deп¬Ғnition 2(Local diп¬Җerential privacy (LDP)). A randomized mechanismM: DвҶ’R with domainDand range Ris (Пө,Оҙ)-locally diп¬Җerentially private for clienti if for all neighboring datasetsDi,DвҖІ i вҲҲD on clienti and for all eventsS вҲҲR in the output space ofM, we have Pr{M(Di) вҲҲS}вүӨ eПөPr{M(DвҖІ i) вҲҲS}+ Оҙ. The deп¬Ғnition of LDP (Deп¬Ғnition 2) is very similar to the original deп¬Ғnition of(Пө,Оҙ)-DP [20, 19], except that now in the FL setting, each client protects its own privacy by encoding and processing its sensitive data locally, and then transmitting the encoded information to the server without coordination and information sharing between the clients. 4Assumptions about the functions. Recalling (1), we consider thenonconvex FL setting, where the functions {fi,j}are arbitrary functions satisfying the following standard smoothness assumption (Assumption 1) and bounded gradient assumption (Assumption 2). Assumption 1(Smoothness). There exists someLвүҘ0, such that for alliвҲҲ[n],j вҲҲ[m], the functionfi,j is L-smooth, i.e., вҲҘвҲҮfi,j(x1) вҲ’вҲҮfi,j(x2)вҲҘвүӨ LвҲҘx1 вҲ’x2вҲҘ, вҲҖx1,x2 вҲҲRd. Assumption 2(Bounded gradient). There exists someGвүҘ0, such that for alliвҲҲ[n],j вҲҲ[m] and xвҲҲRd, we haveвҲҘвҲҮfi,j(x)вҲҘвүӨ G. The smoothness assumption is very standard for the convergence analysis [59, 25, 53, 49], and the bounded gradient assumption is also standard for the diп¬Җerential privacy analysis [6, 69, 32, 23]. 3 Warm-up: Plain Compressed Diп¬Җerentially-Private SGD There are two methods to combine privacy and compression: (1) п¬Ғrst perturb and then compress, and (2) п¬Ғrst compress and then perturb. The advantage of the п¬Ғrst method is that it is very simple and general, since compression will preserve the diп¬Җerential privacy and work seamlessly with any existing privacy mechanisms. However, the second method requires carefully designed perturbation mechanisms (otherwise the perturbation might diminish the communication saving of compression), e.g., binomial perturbation [3] or discrete Gaussian perturbation [36]. In addition, it is observed that the п¬Ғrst method achieves better utility compared with the second one in some settings [17]. Thus, we also apply the п¬Ғrst method in this paper: п¬Ғrst perturb then compress. Baseline algorithm:CDP-SGD. As a warm-up, we п¬Ғrst introduce a simple algorithmCDP-SGD (described in Algorithm 1), which subsumes some existing algorithms as special cases (e.g., [76, 82]) for private FL with better theoretical guarantees. The procedure forCDP-SGD is very simple: at roundt, each clienti п¬Ғrst computes a local stochastic gradientЛңgt i using its local datasetDi (Line 4 in Algorithm 1). Then, it uses Gaussian mechanism [1] to achieve LDP (Line 5 in Algorithm 1) and communicates the compressed perturbed private gradient information to the server (Line 6 in Algorithm 1). Finally, the server aggregates the compressed information and update the model parameters (Line 8вҖ“9 in Algorithm 1). Algorithm 1 Compressed Diп¬Җerentially-Private Stochastic Gradient Descent (CDP-SGD) Input: initial pointx0, stepsizeО·t, varianceПғ2 p, minibatch sizeb 1: for t= 0,1,2,...,T do 2: for each nodeiвҲҲ[n] do in parallel 3: Sample a random minibatchIb from local datasetDi 4: Compute local stochastic gradientЛңgt i = 1 b вҲ‘ jвҲҲIb вҲҮfi,j(xt) // all nodes use SGD method 5: Privacy: gt i = Лңgt i + Оҫt i, whereОҫi t вҲјN(0,Пғ2 pI) 6: Compression: let vt i = Ct i(gt i) and send to the server // direct compression 7: end each node 8: Server aggregates compressed informationvt = 1 n вҲ‘n i=1 vt i 9: xt+1 = xt вҲ’О·tvt 10: end for Theoretical guarantee. We present the theoretical guarantees forCDP-SGD in the following theorem. Theorem 1(Privacy, utility and communication forCDP-SGD). Suppose that Assumptions 1 and 2 hold, and the compression operatorsCt i (cf. Line 6 of Algorithm 1) are drawn independently satisfying Deп¬Ғnition 1. By choosing the algorithm parameters properly and letting the total number of communication rounds T = O ( вҲҡ nLmПө G вҲҡ (1 + Пү)dlog(1/Оҙ) + m2Пө2 dlog(1/Оҙ) ) , 5CDP-SGD (Algorithm 1) satisп¬Ғes(Пө,Оҙ)-LDP and the utility 1 T TвҲ’1вҲ‘ t=0 EвҲҘвҲҮf(xt)вҲҘ2 вүӨO ( G вҲҡ (1 + Пү)Ldlog(1/Оҙ)вҲҡnmПө ) . The proposedCDP-SGD (Algorithm 1) is simple but eп¬Җective. When the compression parameterПү is a constant (i.e., constant compression ratio),CDP-SGD achieves the same utilityO (вҲҡ dlog(1/Оҙ) mПө ) as DP-SGD in the single-node case withn= 1. In comparison, our utility is better than [17] by a factor ofT1/6, and our communication complexity is much better than [76] (see Table 1). However, the communication complexity ofCDP-SGD still has room for improvements due todirect compression (Line 6 in Algorithm 1). In particular, if the size of the local datasetm stored on clients is dominating, thenCDP-SGD (evenifwecomputelocalfullgradientsas CDP-GD)requires O(m2) communication rounds (see Theorem 1), while previous distributed diп¬Җerentially-private algorithms without communication compression (e.g.,Distributed DP-SRM[73], LDP SVRGand LDP SPIDER[54]) only needO(m) communication rounds (see Table 1). 4 SoteriaFL: A Uniп¬Ғed Private FL Framework with Shifted Compres- sion Due to the limitations of plainCDP-SGD, we now present an advanced and uniп¬Ғed private FL framework called SoteriaFL in this section, which allows a large family of local gradient estimators (Line 3 in Algorithm 2 and Line 3вҖ“11 in Algorithm 3). Via adopting the advancedshifted compression(Line 5 in Algorithm 2), SoteriaFL reduces the total number of communication roundsO(m2) of CDP-SGD to O(m), which matches previous uncompressed DP algorithms (see Table 1), and further reduces the total communication complexity due to less communication cost per round. 4.1 A uniп¬Ғed SoteriaFL framework Our SoteriaFL framework is described in Algorithm 2. At roundt, each client will compute a local (stochastic) gradient estimatorЛңgt i using its local datasetDi (Line 3 in Algorithm 2). One can choose several optimization methods for computing this local gradient estimator such as standard gradient descent (GD), stochastic GD (SGD), stochastic variance reduced gradient (SVRG) [34, 43], and SAGA [16] (see e.g., Line 3вҖ“11 in Algorithm 3). Then, each client adds a Gaussian perturbationОҫt i on its gradient estimateЛңgt i to ensure LDP (Line 4 in Algorithm 2). However, diп¬Җerent fromCDP-SGD (Algorithm 1) where we directly compress the perturbed stochastic gradients, now each client maintains a referencest i and compresses the shifted message Лңgt i вҲ’st i (Line 5 in Algorithm 2). This extra shift operation achieves much better convergence behavior (fewer communication rounds) thanCDP-SGD, and thus allowing much lower communication complexity. Algorithm 2 SoteriaFL (a uniп¬Ғed framework for compressed private FL) Input: initial pointx0, stepsizeО·t, shift stepsizeОіt, varianceПғ2 p, initial references0 i = 0 1: for t= 0,1,2,...,T do 2: for each nodeiвҲҲ[n] do in parallel 3: Compute local gradient estimatorЛңgt i // it allows many methods, e.g., SGD, SVRG, and SAGA 4: Privacy: gt i = Лңgt i + Оҫt i, whereОҫt i вҲјN(0,Пғ2 pI) 5: Compression: let vt i = Ct i(gt i вҲ’st i) and send to the server // shifted compression 6: Update shiftst+1 i = st i + ОіtCt i(gt i вҲ’st i) 7: end each node 8: Server aggregates compressed informationvt = st + 1 n вҲ‘n i=1 vt i 9: xt+1 = xt вҲ’О·tvt 10: st+1 = st + Оіt1 n вҲ‘n i=1 vt i 11: end for 64.2 Generic assumption and uniп¬Ғed theory We provide a generic Assumption 3, which is very п¬Ӯexible to capture the behavior of several existing (and potentially new) gradient estimators, while simultaneously maintaining the tractability to enable a uniп¬Ғed and sharp theoretical analysis. Assumption 3(Generic assumption of local gradient estimator forSoteriaFL). The gradient estimatorЛңgt i (Line 3 of Algorithm 2) is unbiasedEt[Лңgt i] = вҲҮfi(xt) for iвҲҲ[n], whereEt takes the expectation conditioned on all history before roundt. Moreover, it can be decomposed into two termsЛңgt i := At i + Bt i and there exist constants GA,GB,C1,C2,C3,C4,Оё and a random sequence{вҲҶt}such that At i = 1 b вҲ‘ jвҲҲIb П•t i,j, Bt i = 1 m mвҲ‘ j=1 ПҲt i,j, (3a) Et [1 n nвҲ‘ i=1 вҲҘЛңgt i вҲ’вҲҮfi(xt)вҲҘ2 ] вүӨC1вҲҶt + C2, (3b) Et [ вҲҶt+1] вүӨ(1 вҲ’Оё)вҲҶt + C3вҲҘвҲҮf(xt)вҲҘ2 + C4EtвҲҘxt+1 вҲ’xtвҲҘ2, (3c) where П•t i,j and ПҲt i,j are bounded byGA and GB respectively, andIb usually denotes a random minibatch with size b. Here,П•t i,j and ПҲt i,j should be viewed as functions related to thej-th sampledi,j stored on clienti. A few comments are in order. Concretely, the decomposition (3a) is used for our uniп¬Ғed privacy analysis (i.e., Theorem 2). We can let one of them be0 if the gradient estimator only contains one term or is not decomposable. The parameters C1 and C2 in (3b) capture the variance of the gradient estimators, e.g., C1 = C2 = 0 if the client computes local full gradientЛңgt i = вҲҮfi(xt), andC1 Мё= 0 (note thatвҲҶt will shrink in (3c)) andC2 = 0 if the client uses variance-reduced gradient estimators such asSVRG/SAGA. Finally, the parameters Оё,C3 and C4 in (3c) capture the shrinking behavior of the variance (incurred by the gradient estimators), where diп¬Җerent variance-reduced gradient methods usually have diп¬Җerent shrinking behaviors. More concrete examples to follow in Lemma 1 in Section 5. Uniп¬Ғed theory for privacy-utility-communication trade-oп¬Җs.Given our generic Assumption 3, we can obtain a uniп¬Ғed analysis forSoteriaFL framework. The following Theorem 2 uniп¬Ғes the privacy analysis and Theorem 3 uniп¬Ғes the utility and communication complexity analysis. Theorem 2(Privacy forSoteriaFL). Suppose that Assumption 3 holds. There exist constantsc and cвҖІ, for any Пө<c вҖІb2T/m2 and ОҙвҲҲ(0,1), SoteriaFL (Algorithm 2) is(Пө,Оҙ)-LDP if we choose Пғ2 p = c(G2 A/4 + G2 B)Tlog(1/Оҙ) m2Пө2 . By further choosing the total number of communication roundsT as T = max { mПө вҲҡ 2(1 + Пү)LОҰ0вҲҡ 3ОІcd(G2 A/4 + G2 B) log(1/Оҙ) , C2m2Пө2 cd(G2 A/4 + G2 B) log(1/Оҙ) } , (5) 7SoteriaFL has the following utility (accuracy) guarantee: 1 T TвҲ’1вҲ‘ t=0 EвҲҘвҲҮf(xt)вҲҘ2 вүӨO ( max {вҲҡ ОІd(G2 A/4 + G2 B) log(1/Оҙ) О·mПө вҲҡ (1 + Пү)L , ОІC2 (1 + Пү)LО· }) . (6) Theorem 3 is a uniп¬Ғed theorem for ourSoteriaFL framework, which covers a large family of local stochastic gradient methods under the generic Assumption 3. In the next Section 5, we will show that many popular local gradient estimators (GD, SGD, SVRG, andSAGA) satisfy Assumption 3, and thus can be captured by our uniп¬Ғed analysis. 5 Some Algorithms within SoteriaFL Framework In this section, we propose several new private FL algorithms (SoteriaFL-GD, SoteriaFL-SGD, SoteriaFL-SVRG and SoteriaFL-SAGA) captured by ourSoteriaFL framework. We give a detailed Algorithm 3 which describes all these fourSoteriaFL-type algorithms in a nutshell. Interestingly,SoteriaFL-style algorithms are more communication-eп¬ғcient thanCDP-SGD when the local dataset size m is large, with a communication complexity ofO(m), in contrast toO(m2) for CDP-SGD. In terms of utility,SoteriaFL-SVRG and SoteriaFL-SAGA can achieve the same utility asCDP-SGD, while SoteriaFL-GD and SoteriaFL-SGD achieve a slightly worse guarantee than that ofCDP-SGD by a factor of 1 + вҲҡП„, whereП„ := (1+Пү)3/2 n1/2 is small when the number of clientsn is large. Gradient complexity of SoteriaFL-style algorithms. Although the utility and the communication complexity are the most important considerations in private FL, another worth-noting criterion is thegradient complexity, which is deп¬Ғned as the total number of stochastic gradients computed by each client. Although SoteriaFL-GD, SoteriaFL-SGD, SoteriaFL-SVRG and SoteriaFL-SAGA have similar communication complexity 9Table 2: Gradient complexity for our proposedSoteriaFL-style algorithms, which is computed as the product of the total number of communication roundsT and the minibatch sizeb. The gradient complexity of SoteriaFL-SVRG/SoteriaFL-SAGA is usually smaller thanSoteriaFL-SGD, and all of them are smaller than SoteriaFL-GD. In sum, we recommendSoteriaFL-SVRG/SoteriaFL-SAGA due to its superior utility and gradient complexity while maintaining almost the same communication complexity asSoteriaFL-SGD/SoteriaFL-GD. 6 Numerical Experiments In this section, we conduct experiments on standard real-world datasets to numerically verify privacy-utility- communication trade-oп¬Җs among diп¬Җerent algorithms. The code can be accessed at:https://github.com/ haoyuzhao123/soteriafl. Concretely, we compare the direct compression algorithmCDP-SGD (Algorithm 1), shifted compression algorithmsSoteriaFL-SGD (Algorithm 3 with Option I) andSoteriaFL-SVRG (Algorithm 3 with Option II), and algorithms without compressionLDP-SGD [1, 54] andLDP-SVRG [54] on two nonconvex problems (logistic regression with nonconvex regularization in Section 6.1 and shallow neural network training in Section 6.2). Experiment setup. In our experiments, we use random-k sparsiп¬Ғcation (see Example 1 in Section 2) as the compression operator, and we setk = вҢҠd 20 вҢӢ, i.e., randomly select 5% coordinates overd dimension to communicate. In other words, the number of communication bitsper roundof uncompressed algorithms equals to that of20 roundsof compressed algorithms. The number of nodesn is 10. For the algorithmic parameters, we tune the stepsizes (learning rates) for all algorithms for each nonconvex problem and select their best ones from the set{0.01,0.03,0.06,0.1,0.3,0.6,1}. Other parameters are set according to their theoretical values. We would like point out that, in order to achieve privacy guarantee, bounded gradient (Assumption 2) is required. However, it is not easy to obtain this upper boundG or it is somewhat large especially for neural networks. Thus, following experiments in previous works [73, 76, 17, 54], we also apply gradient clipping (i.e.clipG(g) = min(1, G вҲҘgвҲҘ) В·g) in our experiments. In particular, we chooseG= 0.5 for logistic regression with nonconvex regularization in Section 6.1 andG= 1 for shallow neural network training in Section 6.2. For the Gaussian perturbationОҫ, we will run experiments for diп¬Җerent levels of(Пө,Оҙ)-LDP guarantee, and compute the variance ofОҫaccording to the theory. 6.1 Logistic regression with nonconvex regularization The п¬Ғrst task is the logistic regression with a nonconvex regularizer, where the objective function over a data sample (a,b) вҲҲD is deп¬Ғned as f(x; (a,b)) := log ( 1 + exp(вҲ’baвҠӨx) ) + О» dвҲ‘ j=1 x2 j 1 + x2 j . From the experimental results, it can be seen that the two uncompressed algorithms (LDP- SGD and LDP-SVRG) converge faster than the three compressed algorithms (CDP-SGD, SoteriaFL-SGD, SoteriaFL-SVRG) in terms ofcommunication rounds(see left columns in each п¬Ғgure). The п¬Ғgures also conп¬Ғrm that shifted compression basedSoteriaFL typically performs better than direct compression basedCDP-SGD in both utility and training loss. Note that here we report the test accuracy for training the neural network instead of the training loss as earlier (see bottom rows in Figures 4вҖ“8 vs. in Figures 1вҖ“3). The takeaways from the experimental results are similar to previous experiments on logistic regression with nonconvex regularization (Figures 1вҖ“ 3). Again, the two uncompressed algorithms (LDP-SGD and LDP-SVRG) converge faster than the three compressed algorithms (CDP-SGD, SoteriaFL-SGD, SoteriaFL-SVRG) in terms ofcommunication rounds(see left columns in each п¬Ғgure), but the gap becomes smaller when the privacy levelПөgets larger (i.e. less privacy guarantee). However, in terms ofcommunication bits(see right columns in each п¬Ғgure), compressed algorithms again perform much better than the uncompressed algorithms, validating the advantage of communication compression schemes. Last but not least, shifted compression basedSoteriaFL-SGD performs better than direct compression basedCDP-SGD in both utility and test accuracy. However, it turns out thatSoteriaFL-SVRG may perform worse thanCDP-SGD for training this shallow neural network. 7 Conclusion We proposeSoteriaFL, a uniп¬Ғed framework for private FL, which accommodates a general family of local gradient estimators including popular stochastic variance-reduced gradient methods and the state-of-the-art shifted compression scheme. A uniп¬Ғed characterization of its performance trade-oп¬Җs in terms of privacy, utility (convergence accuracy), and communication complexity is presented, which is then instantiated to arrive at several new private FL algorithms. All of these algorithms are shown to perform better than the plain CDP-SGD algorithm especially when the local dataset size is large, and have lower communication complexity compared with other private FL algorithms without compression. 15Acknowledgements The work of Z. For the local SVRG estimatorЛңgt i = 1 b вҲ‘ jвҲҲIb (вҲҮfi,j(xt) вҲ’вҲҮfi,j(wt)) + вҲҮfi(wt) (Option II in Algorithm 3), similarly we п¬Ғrst show that it is unbiased as follows, Et пЈ® пЈ°1 b вҲ‘ jвҲҲIb (вҲҮfi,j(xt) вҲ’вҲҮfi,j(wt)) + вҲҮfi(wt) пЈ№ пЈ»= Et пЈ® пЈ°1 b mвҲ‘ j=1 Xi,j(вҲҮfi,j(xt) вҲ’вҲҮfi,j(wt)) + вҲҮfi(wt) пЈ№ пЈ» = 1 m mвҲ‘ j=1 (вҲҮfi,j(xt) вҲ’вҲҮfi,j(wt)) + вҲҮfi(wt) = вҲҮfi(xt) вҲ’вҲҮfi(wt) + вҲҮfi(wt) = вҲҮfi(xt).
