(2017) problem under diп¬Җerential privacy. Note that DME is closely related to the federated averaging (FedAvg) algorithm McMahan et al. (2017a), where in each round, the server updates the global model using a noisy estimate of the mean of local model updates. Such a noisy estimate is typically obtained via a DME mechanism, and thus one can easily build a DP-FedAvg scheme from a DP-DME scheme. Distributed mean estimation under diп¬Җerential privacy Consider nclients each with a data vector xi вҲҲRd that satisп¬Ғes вҲҘxiвҲҘ2 вүӨc (e.g., a clipped local model update). After communicating with nclients, a server releases a noisy estimates ЛҶВөof the mean Вөвүң 1 n вҲ‘ ixi, such that 1) ЛҶВөsatisп¬Ғes a diп¬Җerential privacy constraint (see Deп¬Ғnition 3.1 and Deп¬Ғnition 3.2), and 2) E [ вҲҘЛҶВөвҲ’ВөвҲҘ2 2 ] is minimized. The goal is to design a communication protocol (which includes local encoders and a central decoder) and an estimator ЛҶВө. In this paper, we consider two diп¬Җerent DP settings. The п¬Ғrst is the centralized DP setting: the server has access to all xiвҖҷs, i.e., ЛҶВө= ЛҶВө(x1,...,x n). The second is the distributed DP via SecAgg setting: under this setting, each client is subject to a b-bit communication constraint, so they must п¬Ғrst encode xi into a b-bit message, i.e., Zi = Aenc(xi) вҲҲZ with |Z|вүӨ 2b (See Figure 1 for an illustration). However, instead of directly collecting Z1,...,Z n, the server can only observe the sum of them, so the estimator must be a function of вҲ‘n i=1 Zi (i.e., ЛҶВө = ЛҶВө(вҲ‘n i=1 Zi)). Moreover, we require that the sum вҲ‘ iZi satisп¬Ғes DP (which is stronger than requiring ЛҶВөto be DP), meaning that individual information will not be disclosed to the server as well. Notice that since SecAgg operates on a п¬Ғnite additive group, we require Zto have an additive structure. Without loss of generality, we will set Zto be (ZM)m for some m,M вҲҲN, where ZM denotes the group of integers modulo M (equipped with modulo M addition) and mis the dimension of the space we are projecting onto. In 7Figure 1: Private mean estimation via SecAgg. other words, we allocate log M bits for every coordinate of the projected vector. Note that in this case, the total per-client communication cost is b= mlog M. For a п¬Ғxed privacy constraint, the fundamental problem we seek to solve is: what is the smallest communication cost under the distributed DP setting needed to achieve the accuracy of the centralized DP setting? Further, we seek to discover schemes that (a) achieve the optimal privacy-accuracy-communication trade-oп¬Җs and (b) are memory eп¬ғcient and computationally fast in encoding and decoding. 5 Communication Cost of DME with SecAgg In this section, we characterize the optimal communication cost under the distributed DP via SecAgg setting, deп¬Ғned as the smallest number of bits (as a function of n,d,Оө ) needed to achieve the same accuracy (up to a constant factor) of the centralized setting under the same ( Оө,Оҙ)-DP constraint. Optimal MSE under central DP We start by specifying the optimal accuracy under a fully trusted server and no communication constraints. Under a DME setting where вҲҘxiвҲҘ2 вүӨc for all iвҲҲ[n], the в„“2 sensitivity of the mean query Вө(x1,...,x n) вүң 1 n вҲ‘n i=1 xi is bounded by вҲҶ (Вө) вүң max xn,xвҖІ 1 оө№оө№Вө(x1,...,x n) вҲ’Вө(xвҖІ 1,...,x n) оө№оө№2 2 вүӨ2c2 n . Therefore, to achieve (Оө,Оҙ) central DP, the server can add coordinate-wise independent Gaussian noise to Вө. This gives an в„“2 error that scales as OОҙ ( c2d n2Оө2 ) , which is known to be tight in the high privacy regime Kamath & Ullman (2020). Moreover, the resulting estimator is unbiased3. Communication costs of DDG Next, we examine the communication costs of previous dis- tributed DP schemes such as the DDG mechanism. As mentioned in Section 3.2 and Theorem E.1, in order to achieve the OОҙ ( c2d n2Оө2 ) error, the communication cost of DDG must to be at least Оҳ ( dlog ( d/Оө2)) bits. Note that the communication cost scales up with 1 /Оө because in the high- privacy regimes, the noise variance needs to be increased accordingly to provide stronger DP. Thus SecAggвҖҷs group size needs to be enlarged to capture the larger signal range and avoid catastrophic modular clipping errors. A similar phenomenon occurs for other additive noise-based mechanisms, e.g, the Skellam and binomial Agarwal et al. (2018, 2021) mechanisms. 3Notice that in this work, we are mostly interest in unbiased estimators (see Remark 5.5 for a discussion). 8However, we show that the Оҳ ( dlog ( d/Оө2)) cost is strictly sub-optimal. In particular, the linear dependency on d can be further improved when n2Оө2 вүӘd. To demonstrate this, we start by the following example to show that under a ( Оө,Оҙ) central DP constraint, one can reduce the dimensionality to m= O(n2Оө2) without harming the MSE. Dimensionality reduction under central DP Consider the following simple project-and- perturb mechanism: 1. Dimensionality reduction with SecAgg The above example shows that with a random pro- jection, we can reduce the dimensionality from d to O(n2Оө2) without increasing the MSE too much. Lower bounds for private DME with SecAgg Next, we complement our achievability result in Theorem 5.2 with a matching communication lower bound. Our lower bound indicates that Algorithm 2 is indeed optimal (in terms of communication eп¬ғciency), hence characterizing the fundamental privacy-communication-accuracy trade-oп¬Җs. The lower bound leverages the fact that under SecAgg, the individual communication budget each client has is equal to the total number of bits the server can observe, which are all equal to the cardinality of the п¬Ғnite group Zthat SecAgg acts on. Therefore, even if each client sends a b-bit message (so the total information transmitted is nВ·b bits), the server still can only observe b bits information. Notice that in this work, we are mostly interested in unbiased estimators due to the following two reasons: 1) it largely facilities the convergence analysis of the SGD based methods, as these types of stochastic п¬Ғrst-order methods usually assume access to an unbiased gradient estimator in each round. 2) In the high-dimensional or high-privacy regimes where d вү«n2Оө2, the MSE is not the right performance measure since an estimator can have a large bias while still achieving a relative small MSE. In order to rule out these impractical schemes, we hence impose the unbiasedness constraint. 6 Sparse DME with SecAgg and DP Theorem 5.2 in Section 5 speciп¬Ғes the optimal trade-oп¬Җs of private DME for all possible datasets xn. In other words, it provides a worst-case (over all possible xi) bound on the utility and shows that Algorithm 2 is worst-case optimal. However, we show in this section that with additional assumptions on the data, it is possible to improve the trade-oп¬Җs beyond what is given in Theorem 5.2. One such assumption is sparsity of the data, which is justiп¬Ғed by several empirical results that gradients tend to be (or are close to being) sparse. However, one can extend the similar analysis in Section 5 and obtain the results for slog dвү«n2Оө2 regime. 7 Empirical Analysis We run experiments on the full Federated EMNIST and Stack Overп¬Ӯow datasets (Caldas et al., 2018), two common benchmarks for FL tasks. F-EMNIST has 62 classes and N = 3400 clients with a total of 671 ,585 training samples. Inputs are single-channel (28 ,28) images. The Stack Overп¬Ӯow (SO) dataset is a large-scale text dataset based on responses to questions asked on the site Stack Overп¬Ӯow. The are over 10 8 data samples unevenly distributed across N = 342477 clients. We focus on the next word prediction (NWP) task: given a sequence of words, predict the next words in the sequence. On both datasets, we select nвҲҲ[100,1000] and R= 1500. On F-EMNIST, we experiment with a вүҲ106 parameter (4 layer) Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) used by Kairouz et al. (2021a). On SONWP, we experiment with a вүҲ4 В·106 parameter (4 layer) long-short term memory (LSTM) modelвҖ”the same as prior work Andrew et al. (2021); Kairouz et al. (2021a). In both cases, clients train for 1 local epoch using SGD. Only the server uses momentum. For distributed DP, we use the geometric adaptive clipping of Andrew et al. (2021). We use the same procedure as Kairouz et al. (2021a). We randomly rotate vectors using the Discrete Fourier Transform. We use their hyperparameter values for conditional randomized rounding and modular clipping. We communicate 16 bits per parameter for F-EMNIST and 18 for SONWP unless otherwise indicated. We repeat all experiments with 5 diп¬Җerent seeds (or more, where stated). We provide full detail on the models, datasets, and training setups in Appendix B, as well as the chosen values of the noise multiplier and sparse random projections (via sketching) parameters in Appendix C. We provide details of our algorithms for sparse random projections (via sketching) in Appendix D. Analyzing the privacy-utility-communication tradeoп¬Җ We now study the best compression rates that we can attain without signiп¬Ғcantly impacting the current performance. For this, we train models that achieve state-of-the-art performance on the FL tasks we consider, and allow a slack of вҲҶ = 4% relative to these models when trained without compression ( r= 1x). We п¬Ғrst consider z= 0, i.e., no DP, and see that about r= 4x compression can be attained. However, recall that our theoretical results suggest that as z increases and thus Оө decreases, fewer bits of communication are needed. Our experiments echo this п¬Ғnding: at z = 0.3, we observe r = 5x is now attainable and at z вүҘ0.5, r= 10x is attainable. The highest z we display can correspond both to a tighter privacy regime (Оө вүҲ10 or less) but, importantly also to models that are still highly performant indicating that a practitioner could reasonably select both these z and r to train models comparable to the state-of-the-art. Our experiments on F-EMNIST in Figure 2b show similar п¬Ғndings where п¬Ғxing rвүҘ50x can attain zвүҘ0.5 for вҖҳfreeвҖҷ. Quantization or dimensionality reduction? Though we achieve signiп¬Ғcant compression rates from our linear dimensionality reduction technique, we now explore how these results compare with compression via quantization. Theoretically our bound can achieve a minimum compression independent of the ambient gradient size d. Because of this, we also expect our methods to outperform those based on quantization because their communication scales with d. Our results in Figure 3 and Table 1 corroborate this hypothesis. We compare our compression against conditional randomized rounding to an integer grid of п¬Ғeld size 2 b. We vary the quantization (bits) per parameter bwhile allowing the same вҲҶ as above. Combined with dimensionality reduction, this gives a total communication per ambient parameter as b/r. When we use the vanilla distributed DP scheme of Kairouz et al. (2021a), we п¬Ғnd that we can only compress to 10 bits per parameter on SONWP; if we instead favour our linear dimensionality reduction technique, we achieve a much lower 1.2 bits per parameter (with r= 10x and b= 12). For F-EMNIST, we п¬Ғnd we can compress down to 0.24 bits per parameter at z = 0.5 by optimizing both b and r; this is much lower than using only quantization (10 bits per parameter) and a marginal increase over only dimensionality reduction (0.27). The results in the п¬Ғnal column take the form mean Вұstandard deviation. 8 Conclusion In this paper, we study the optimal privacy-communication-accuracy trade-oп¬Җs under distributed DP via SecAgg. We show that existing schemes are order optimal when dвүӘn2Оө2 and strictly sub- optimal otherwise. To address this issue, we provide an optimal scheme that leverages sparse random projections. We also show how our scheme can be minimally modiп¬Ғed when the client updates are sparse to further improve the trade-oп¬Җs. Our extensive experiments on FL benchmark datasets demonstrate signiп¬Ғcant communication gains ( вҲј10x) relative to existing schemes. Many important questions remain open, including obtaining a fundamental characterization of the privacy-accuracy- communication trade-oп¬Җs under other models of distributed DP (e.g. via a trusted third-party or in a secure enclave as in Bittau et al. Full results for Figure 3 and 5. 23A.1 Impact of cohort size Finally, we explore how varying the number of clients per round (or, cohort size) n impacts the privacy-utility-communication tradeoп¬Җ. This value plays several key roles in this tradeoп¬Җ. First, increasing n increases the sampling probability of the cohort, which increases the total privacy expenditure. However, it also tends to improve model performanceвҖ”this may mean that a higher noise multiplier z can be chosen so as to instead decrease the total privacy cost (this is typically the case when N is large enough, e.g., SO). In terms of communication, Theorem 5.2 suggests that increasing n will also increase the per-client comunication. Because of the aforementioned complex tradeoп¬Җs, we (approximately) п¬Ғx the privacy budget Оө and only perturb n minimally around a nominal value of 100. In Figure 7, we see that dependence of communication on n is observed empirically as well. In addition to this impact on r, setting n can also have a signiп¬Ғcant impact on the run time of SecAgg, of O(nlog(n)d). For large values of n, this can entirely prevent the protocol from completing. Because a practitioner desires the most performant model, a common goal is the increase n so as to obtain a tight Оө (due to a now higher z) with the least cost in performance. But, because large n can crash SecAgg, this places a constraint on the maximum n that can be chosen. Since our methods compress the updates ( d above), it is possible to still increase n so long as we increase r accordingly (by n2 n1 log n2 log n1 where n2 >n1), which maintains п¬Ғxed runtime. If the resulting model at higher n achieves higher performance, then we observe a net beneп¬Ғt from this tradeoп¬Җ. For a practical privacy parameter or z = 0.6, our results in Table 8 suggest that this may be possible. Speciп¬Ғcally, increasing n from 100 вҶ’1000 and settings r= 50x> 15x accordingly, we observe that the п¬Ғnal model with n= 1000 clients achieves a nearly 5pp gain. We train models either by sketching the entire concatenated gradient vector or by sketching the вҖҳembeddingвҖҷ layer separate from the вҖҳrestвҖҷ of the model. 27B Datasets and Training Setup We run experiments on the full Federated EMNIST and Stack Overп¬Ӯow datasets (Caldas et al., 2018), two common benchmarks for FL tasks. F-EMNIST has 62 classes and N = 3400 clients, with each user holding both a train and test set of examples. In total, there are 671 ,585 training examples and 77,483 test examples. Inputs are single-channel (28 ,28) images. We sample nвҲҲ[100,1000] clients per round for a total R= 1500 rounds. The Stack Overп¬Ӯow (SO) dataset is a large-scale text dataset based on responses to questions asked on the site Stack Overп¬Ӯow. The are over 10 8 data samples unevenly distributed across N = 342477 clients. We focus on the next word prediction (NWP) task: given a sequence of words, predict the next words in the sequence. We sample use nвҲҲ[100,1000] and R= 1500. On F-EMNIST, we experiment with a вүҲ1 million parameter (4 layer) Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) used by (Kairouz et al., 2021a). On SONWP, we experiment with a вүҲ4 million parameter (4 layer) long-short term memory (LSTM) model, which is the same as prior work Andrew et al. (2021); Kairouz et al. (2021a). On F-EMNIST, we use a server learning rate of 1 . normalized by n (the number of clients) and momentum of 0.9 (Polyak, 1964); the client uses a learning rate of 0 .01 without momentum. On Stack Overп¬Ӯow, we use a server learning rate of 1 .78 normalized by n and momentum of 0.9; the client uses a learning rate of 0 .3. For distributed DP, we use the geometric adaptive clipping of Andrew et al. (2021) with an initial в„“2 clipping norm of 0 .1 and a target quantile of 0 .5. We use the same procedure as Kairouz et al. (2021a) and п¬Ӯatten using the Discrete Fourier Transform, pick ОІ = exp (вҲ’0.5) as the conditional randomized rounding bias, and use a modular clipping target probability of 6 .33eвҲ’5 or вүҲ4 standard deviations at the server (assuming normally distributed updates). We communicate 16 bits per parameter for F-EMNIST and 18 bits for SONWP unless otherwise indicated. On F-EMNIST, our вҖҳlargeвҖҷ model corresponds to the CNN whereas our вҖҳsmallвҖҷ model corresponds to an вүҲ200,000 parameter model with 3 dense layers (see Figure 12). (2020); Mironov (2017), our highest noise multipliers roughly correspond to Оө = {5,10}using Оҙ= 1/N and privacy ampliп¬Ғcation via п¬Ғxed batch sampling. Linear compression We display results in terms of the noise multiplier which fully speciп¬Ғes the Оө-DP given our other parameters ( n, N, and R). We discuss this choice in Appendix C. We use a sparse random projection (i.e. a count sketch together with a mean estimator) which compresses gradients to a sketch matrix of size ( length,width). We test length вҲҲ{10,15,20,25}and п¬Ғnd that 15 leads to optimal п¬Ғnal test performance. We use this value for all our experiments and calculate the width= d/(rВ·length) where gradient вҲҲRd and r is the compression rate. We normalize each sketch row by the length to lower the clipping norm, п¬Ғnding some improvements in our results. Thus, decoding requires only summing the gradient estimate from each row. We provide the full algorithms in below in Appendix D. D Linear Compression In Practice Algorithm 4 Gradient Count-Mean Sketch Encoding. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Defines and characterizes the fundamental communication cost for differentially private federated learning under secure aggregation (SecAgg). Proves that to match the accuracy of centralized DP (Gaussian mechanism) for distributed mean estimation, the optimal per-client communication is Г•(min(n^2 Оө^2, d)) bits, which is both sufficient (achievability) and necessary (lower bounds) for unbiased estimators. Introduces a SecAgg-compatible linear scheme using sparse random projections combined with the distributed discrete Gaussian (DDG) mechanism achieving this bound. Extends to sparse mean estimation, achieving logarithmic dependence on model dimension with communication O(s log d log(n^2 + s log d/Оө^2)) and MSE O(c^2 s log^2 d/(n^2 Оө^2)). Demonstrates large empirical communication savings (вүҲ10Г—), reaching as low as 0.24вҖ“1.2 bits per parameter without test-time performance loss on real FL tasks.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Problem setting: Distributed Mean Estimation (DME) with per-client updates x_i вҲҲ R^d, ||x_i||_2 вүӨ c, SecAgg ensures the server sees only the sum in a finite group; aim to achieve centralized-DP-level MSE O(c^2 d/(n^2 Оө^2)).
- Achievability (Algorithm 2): Linear compression via sparse random projection S (count-sketch/sparse JL) to m = Оҳ(n^2 Оө^2) dimensions, client encodes y_i = S x_i with DDG (clipping ~1.1c, modulus M, granularity Оі, noise Пғ), SecAgg sums, server decodes DDG to obtain \hat{Ој}_y and unprojects with S^T. Error decomposed into clipping, compression, and privatization errors; bounded using JL lemmas and an unsketching norm lemma. Privacy analyzed via RDP of DDG; per-client bits O(m log(n + вҲҡ(n^3 Оө^2/m) + вҲҡ(m)/Оө)) = O(n^2 Оө^2 log n).
- Lower bounds: (i) General compression lower bound via covering numbers: with target MSE Оҫ, need log|Z| = О©(d log(c^2/Оҫ)). (ii) For unbiased schemes, information-theoretic bound using quantized Fisher information (Barnes et al., 2020) yields MSE вүҘ C c^2 d/b, giving per-client bits вүҘ О©(min(n^2 Оө^2, d)) to reach centralized-DP MSE. Key SecAgg insight: server observes only the sum, so total observable information equals per-client bit budget.
- Sparse case (Algorithm 3): Use Gaussian measurement matrix S with m = Оҳ(s log d), encode with DDG using adjusted clipping cвҖІ = cВ·Пғ_max(S), server decodes \hat{Ој}_y then solves LASSO min_x { (1/m)||\hat{Ој}_y вҲ’ Sx||_2^2 + О»||x||_1 } with О» = O(c log d/(n Оө)). Analysis via (soft) Restricted Eigenvalue and Lasso oracle inequalities yields MSE O(c^2 s log^2 d/(n^2 Оө^2)); per-client communication O(s log d В· log(n^2 + s log d/Оө^2)).
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Datasets: Federated EMNIST (62 classes; NвүҲ3,400 clients; 671,585 train, 77,483 test). Stack Overflow Next-Word Prediction (NвүҲ342,477 clients; large-scale text).
- Training protocol: R=1500 rounds; cohort size n вҲҲ [100, 1000]; clients train 1 local epoch with SGD; server uses momentum; adaptive clipping per Andrew et al. (2021). DDG preprocessing uses DFT rotation, conditional randomized rounding bias ОІ=exp(вҲ’0.5), modular clipping target probability вүҲ6.33eвҲ’5.
- Models: F-EMNIST CNN вүҲ1M params (plus a вүҲ200k param small DNN for comparison); Stack Overflow LSTM вүҲ4M params.
- Communication baselines: 16 bits/parameter (F-EMNIST) and 18 bits/parameter (Stack Overflow) without compression. Evaluate compression rate r (dimensionality reduction) and quantization bits per parameter b; total communication = b/r bits per parameter. Privacy controlled by noise multiplier z (mapped to Оө via RDP accounting assumptions).
- Evaluation: Final test accuracy with allowable slack О” (F-EMNIST: 1%; Stack Overflow: 4%) relative to uncompressed DP baseline; multiple seeds (вүҘ5, up to 30). Also study effect of cohort size n and compare against quantization-only.
- Results: Higher privacy (larger z, smaller Оө) enables higher compression. Achieved rвүҘ10Г— at zвүҘ0.5. Stack Overflow: as low as 1.2 bits/parameter (r=10Г—, b=12) with no accuracy loss. F-EMNIST: as low as 0.24 bits/parameter at z=0.5 by jointly optimizing r and b; also 0.7 (z=0.3) and 1.2 (z=0.1) bits/parameter. Per-layer sketching and thresholding did not improve results. Larger (compressed) models outperform smaller models with similar latent sizes.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- The strongest lower bound (О©(min(n^2 Оө^2, d))) relies on unbiasedness; bounds for biased estimators are weaker in high-dimensional/high-privacy regimes, though unbiasedness is standard for SGD convergence.
- Communication gains over prior DDG are most pronounced when d вү« n^2 Оө^2; in the opposite regime the per-client cost remains Оҳ(d log(d/Оө^2)).
- Requires linear encoders compatible with SecAgg, excluding potentially more powerful nonlinear compression (e.g., sparsification with thresholding, entropy coding).
- Sparse variant needs knowledge (or tuning) of sparsity s and regularization О», and solves LASSO, adding computational complexity; uses Gaussian projections rather than extremely sparse sketches.
- Experiments limited to two FL benchmarks and specific architectures; do not explore broader domains, extreme client heterogeneity, or high dropout/failure rates.
- Privacy reporting uses a noise multiplier z with approximate mapping to (Оө, Оҙ) that depends on sampling assumptions; exact Оө, Оҙ not always explicitly tabulated.
- Practical SecAgg constraints require minimum bitwidth per coordinate (О©(log n)), which can limit ultra-low b in practice; SecAgg runtime can still be a bottleneck for large n (though compression helps).
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Extend fundamental privacy-communication-accuracy trade-offs to other distributed DP models (trusted third party, secure enclaves, shuffle models, multi-server SecAgg) and to settings with client dropouts/adversaries.
- Develop tighter lower bounds that do not rely on unbiasedness or that use alternative loss metrics beyond MSE; study optimality for heterogeneous client distributions and non-Euclidean norms.
- Design adaptive/data-dependent but SecAgg-compatible linear projections (structured or per-layer) and dynamic bit allocation across rounds/coordinates.
- Provide end-to-end convergence analyses of DP-FedAvg with sketching across many rounds and optimizers; characterize interaction with adaptive clipping and momentum.
- Create more efficient, parameter-free sparse decoders (e.g., iterative thresholding) under SecAgg constraints; explore learning-based linear compressors with shared randomness.
- Broaden empirical validation to more modalities and larger models; measure latency/energy on-device and large-scale deployment robustness.
- Joint optimization of quantization and projection under DP constraints; heterogeneity-aware cohort sizing and compression scheduling.
- Robust protocols for high dropout rates and byzantine behavior while preserving SecAgg and DP guarantees.
